This way, it also hints at being able to add commands without importing their modules, but wouldn't actually enable that.

Regarding the scheduler, I've started work on extracting out the scheduler, but as soon as I did, I ran up against the client's own use of the scheduler. In particular, the `set_keepalive` method calls `execute_every` to cause the call to be invoked periodically. Without some sort of scheduler in place, this function will fail. Therefore, the implementation has an intrinsic dependency on a scheduler, so it seems not possible to leave the scheduler as an implementation detail of the consumer.

The use case for `writable` is when you have a latency-sensitive protocol, and you want to delay making a decision about which bits to send until the kernel is ready to accept them - see #83. For example, imagine a screen-sharing protocol that loops on taking a screenshot and sending it -- if the network isn't ready to accept data, then you want to delay taking the screenshot until it is, rather than queue up stale data.

The use case for `readable` is very specific: if you have an HTTP client with a connection pool, then after using a connection you put it back in the pool and ignore it for a while. Later, when you decide to make another request to the same server, you pull the connection back out again to re-use. However, in the mean time, the server might have gotten bored and closed the connection (possibly after sending some `408 Request Timeout` response). So when retrieving a connection from the pool, you want to do something like:

1. check if the socket is readable. If so, the server has given up. This isn't an error; it just means we need to close this connection and make a new one.
2. Once we have a connection that seems to be valid, send our request, while simultaneously listening for a response. At this point, an early response or socket close indicates an error → this request failed.

So you really do want a non-blocking "is there data there to read?" query here. In my [efforts to port urllib3 to Trio](https://github.com/njsmith/urllib3/issues/1), my plan so far is to just [drop down to the lower level socket API here](https://github.com/njsmith/urllib3/blob/bleach-spike/urllib3/backends/trio_backend.py#L66-L75) and [call `select` or whatever](https://github.com/njsmith/urllib3/blob/bleach-spike/urllib3/backends/_util.py#L18-L22), since this is such a weird and specialized thing that I don't want to put it into my abstract stream API.

Note in particular that for a TLS-encrypted connection, there isn't in general any way to check if the *logical, application-level data stream* is readable without actually trying to read data, but in this case that's not what we want anyway – here we really just want to check if there's any data on the underlying socket level.

That time.sleep thing is a really interesting bug! I think what's going on
is that Python assumes that on unix, syscalls like sleep will get
interrupted if a signal arrives, and if you look at the source to
time.sleep there's some logic there to notice this interruption, run the
signal handler, and then continue the sleep (unless the signal handler
raised an exception, in which case the exception propagates).

But here, the kernel is (for whatever reason) delivering the signal to the
child thread, rather than the main thread. So the C level signal handler is
running promptly, and it sets the relevant flags to tell the interpreter to
run the python level handler asap, but the call to sleep is *not*
interrupted, so the main thread doesn't wake up and notice these flags
until later.

On Windows Python has some special logic in sleep (and a few other places,
but not select) to deal with this kind of thing, because on Windows signals
are always delivered in separate threads. But on unix the rule is that the
kernel gets to pick any thread to handle the signal, and Python is
incorrectly assuming it will always be the main thread.

At least that's my guess.

A possible workaround would be to call pthread_sigmask in the child thread
to block delivery of SIGCHLD. (Python exposes pthread_sigmask in the signal
module.) Then the kernel won't be able to deliver the signal to the child
thread, and will have to use the main thread instead.

Doing this for every thread is fragile and error prone though, because you
have to do it for *every* thread. And libraries like zmq might spawn their
own threads without our knowledge. Really this is a python bug, I think. I
wouldn't be surprised if there's some report on bugs.python.org from like
2009 complaining about it :-).

just some food for thought from adding a [small verb](https://github.com/tidyverse/tidyr/pull/201/files) verb in tidr and what I liked about the dplyr/tidr implementation:

* I also like the extensibility of single dispatch (=S3 system in R, `@singledispatch` in python) to make the functions work with different inputs. Instead of doing `if instanceof(input, Series): ... elif instanceof(input, GroupBy): ... else raise Error()` you write a simple base functions which just raises `NotImplementedError` and decorate is with `@singledispatch` and then add the rest of the functions for Series, GroupBy and so on. Extending the system for SQL backends is then "only" adding a functions for suitable wrapper around a SQL connection. I wrote a small [protoype for it](https://janschulz.github.io/python-data-pipelines.html) and still want to try it in dfply.
* The strict split between "Non-standard-evaluation" (NSE -> all normal dplyr functions) and the SE variants (the functions with underscore), where the later are for "programming" and the former for "interactive" work (see https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/dplyr/vignettes/nse.html). In the python world, it basically translates to: NSE functions do the stuff to get a list of columns and then call the SE functions with that list of columns. IMO the split of the decorator between "do all the work to get the list of columns and the step of doing something with that list of columns made sense as it lets you "opt in" to some features: I like composability by explictly adding a decorator or (IMO even better) by calling a function.

I will fix this at some point, because it's obviously intuitive to use it that way (and several users are coming over from PyPDF2), but a regular string is not really a valid `PdfDict` key, even though it sorta kinda works sometimes.  The only valid `PdfDict` keys are `PdfName` objects.  If you really want to use `get()` you can use `PdfName.Annots` or `PdfName('/Annots')`, but the only reason to _ever_ use `get` would be for efficiency -- e.g. if you are not caching those `PdfName` objects for multiple `get()` operations, don't do it.

The _right_ way to do it is simply `xxx.Annots`.  This will always return `None` if `Annots` is not defined in the dict, because that's how dictionaries work in PDF files.

Hi,
that's a "feature"of AD, when a group exceed the 1000 members limit AD use a quite "obscure" feature of the LDAP protocol that is called "range tag". The problem is that usually until you don't reach that limit you are unaware of it. So your working code breaks without any apparent reason.

ldap3 should handle this for you, try to use the ``auto_range=True`` parameter in the Connection object and the search operation is transparently repeated until the range is exhausted. Your code should work again as before. The "OPER_" prefix is inserted by the ldap3 lilbrary because the attribute name "member" has been changed by the server. It should disappear with the auto_range parameter.

Let me know if this works for you.

Bye,
Giovanni

The author of the module chose to treat `.call()` as a special attribute
because the usefulness of being able to create method callers
outweighs the usefulness of being able to use a very specific attribute getter.

You can subclass underscore to redefine `.call()` as an attribute getter.
You can also subclass underscore to turn attribute getters into method callers.
In no way can this be perfect because this is a hack build on top of python's [data model](https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html).

Hey, sorry I forgot about this issue.

> If not, the first point is compared to the third point. This is where the mistake is.
>
> Measuring the distance from the first point to the third point doesn't follow the path, unless all the points follow a straight line.

Well, this is just one of the possible ways to "clean" the track. I'm not saying that it's the right way, but it also makes sense. If your track have points every 10cm, but you want them every 5m then this, I think, makes sense. Even for your track to me it makes sense, the only problem (looking to your images) is that  the treshold is too big -- but the basic shape of the track is preserved.

If you want to reduce the number of points in your track, but preserve the most characteristic part of its shape (i.e. remove points where the tracks points are in line, but preserve curves and extreemes) -- you should use the `simplify()` method. It will not indiscriminately remove points with a certain treshold of distance from the previous point but takes shape in account.

To paraphrase @MinchinWeb, `colorama.Fore.WHITE` is typically grey on both Windows and Linux, whereas `colorama.Style.BRIGHT + colorama.Fore.WHITE` (bright white) is typically real white on both. i.e. what you're seeing is intended. If you don't see this behavior on your linux machines, it may be because of your terminal's color settings - which may set "dim white" (grey) to actual white.
Unfortunately, `colorama.Style.BRIGHT` doesn't affect the background so you can't use it to achieve a "bright white" (real white) background.
You can try using `colorama.Back.LIGHTWHITE_EX` instead - e.g. `print colorama.Back.LIGHTWHITE_EX + "PASS" + colorama.Back.RESET`.

Changing the implementation to always print the codes may be a break from the current philosophy, but not from the current behavior. If we fix this (autoreset), then colorama will continue to work as it does now, and will print colors in output redirections, so no applications will break.

btw, here's a quick look at others:
* `termcolor` (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/termcolor) also print the colors even if the output handle is not a tty
* `logbook` handle this (although they don't use colorama) in the application level: https://github.com/mitsuhiko/logbook/blob/00b3ba9c1eb92927c3260f7cc03f02cd5c0ed1d7/logbook/more.py#L297

I'm having a similar issue. `OctetString` doesn't like to be `.debug()`'d. Here's a simplified example.

```python
def test_octect_string():
        import asn1crypto.core
        from binascii import unhexlify

        der_octetstring = unhexlify("0402CCE9")
        a = asn1crypto.core.OctetString.load(der_octetstring)

        print("!")
        print(a.dump())
        #print(a.debug())

        b = asn1crypto.core.OctetString(unhexlify("CCE9"))
        assert b.dump() == a.dump()
        #b.debug()
```

This code works fine as-is, but if you uncomment any of the `.debug` calls then it explodes with:

```python
    UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xcc in position 2: ordinal not in range(128)
```


Now, I'm not sure if I'm using OctentString here correctly, but unfortunately I'm working with an ASN.1 schema and data source that I don't control, and it's using it like this:

```asn1
KeyBlock ::= Sequence
{
    KeyBlock version = 1 (INTEGER)
    Keys ::= Set
    {
        KeyInfo ::= Sequence
        {
            TR31Key = (PRINTABLESTRING)
            keyType = (INTEGER)
            ksn = (OCTET STRING)
            keySlot = (INTEGER)
            keyName = (PRINTABLESTRING)
            keyCheck = (OCTET STRING)
        }
    }
}
```

with `0xCCE9` for `keyCheck`, which causes problems on `debug()`.

(Though [this](http://luca.ntop.org/Teaching/Appunti/asn1.html) guide says OctectStrings are any 8bit data values, not necessarily ascii-printable ones)

```
echo 123123 > /var/www/challenges/test.txt && curl http://yourdomain/.well-known/acme-challenge/test.txt
```

If it outputs 123123 then the problem is with acme_tiny. If not, the problem is with your configuration.

@evansd: Your comment implies that referencing files by the original names is *incorrect* - I disagree! There are absolutely use cases for having Django as your backend (and using it to manage static files) that don't involve using it to dynamically create your front-end pages. For example, you might have a mobile app with bundled front-end code that uses the Django backend and needs to access static files by their regular names. `whitenoise` currently has good support for this scenario, since you can rely on the versioned files being cached forever while using `WHITENOISE_MAX_AGE` to control the HTTP caching for the regular flies.

I think it's fine for you to suggest turning on this option in the documentation if you think it represents a good optimization for a common use case, my point is just that this isn't a backwards-compatibility issue, or a new vs old project issue, it's a matter of choosing a default that works for all use cases.

@pavlov99 Thanks for such a prompt reply. It seems to me though that json-rpc has a responsibility to meet its guarantee and return valid JSON. Every application is susceptible to this issue. (Incidentally, it also seems that the issue isn't limited to the Django backend as you will see below.)

In [this function](https://github.com/pavlov99/json-rpc/blob/f35f16bccbff148210bbf5496fc8190546940ca6/jsonrpc/manager.py#L114), `JSONRPCResponseManager` constructs a `JSONRPCServerError` when an exception occurs. However, it makes the assumption that `e.args` is JSON serializable, which isn't necessarily the case. It seems json-rpc shouldn't be creating a `JSONRPCError` object there if it can't guarantee that the object is actually serializable.

Just a little bit more information on this one.

Navigating to page [JAC](http://www.nfl.com/teams/roster?team=JAC) returns a status of ['301'](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_301) "Moved Permanently" and redirects to [JAX](http://www.nfl.com/teams/roster?team=JAX).

`nflgame` is not doing anything with the redirect.  It simply sees that it didn't get a status of `'200'` and returns `None`.  

An identical status is returned for [STL](http://www.nfl.com/teams/roster?team=STL) which now redirects to [LA](http://www.nfl.com/teams/roster?team=LA).

There is a lot of information related to your questions in the issue tracker. I'd recommend running a few searches.

With that said, your first line of defense is to delete the offending the JSON and call `nflgame.one` for the corresponding game. It will re-download the JSON. On occasion, it will be fixed. When it is not fixed, there is not much we can do and it is outside the scope of nflgame. nflgame is meant to provide a convenient API to the JSON data. If the underlying data is flawed, then so is nflgame.

I thought `click` events were still fired on touch devices. Anyway, in anticipation of future support for [pointer events](https://github.com/jquery/PEP) I'll add three new options which will allow changing the sort triggering events:

* `pointerClick` - mouse click event (not really part of the pointer events). Additional custom events can be included in this option with event names separated by spaces (e.g. `'click makeItSo'`)
* `pointerDown` - pointer down (mouse or touch) event. This event only saves the current header cell being activated for a sort. 
* `pointerUp` - pointer up (mouse or touch) event. This event triggers a sort if the target of this event and the pointer down event match.

If you want to trigger a sort when the pointer down event fires, then set the `pointerClick` option to that event; Also set the `pointerDown` and `pointerUp` options to empty strings.

**Warning**: Do not set the `pointerDown` or `pointerUp` options to include multiple events of the same action. For example, if the `pointerDown` option is set to `"mousedown pointerdown"`, both events fire asynchronously and since tablesorter does not check for a time interval between events, both could potentially trigger a sort making the table column appear to not sort at all (if already sorted).

Thanks for the update. The reason `divisor` is showing up is that you have it declared explicitly using a `<global>` tag and you have assigned it as a `<target>`. You should change the name of the target to `[default]` for functions to save the actual function output. For example:

```xml
<target name="[default]" compareto="result.out" varfile="polya.out" when="each" />
```

As far as the compilation error goes, it appears that the explicit interface for the function is not visible to the program. There are two possibilities:

1. fortpy didn't add the `public` keyword in the driver it generated to make `gcd_2ints` publicly visible.
2. The function is part of an interface in the module; the interface is public. Since it belongs to the interface, fortran may not allow it to *also* be declared as a public function for the module.

Some background to how loopdiagram and hvacbuilder came about.

The first diagrams were drawn by hand using paper and pencil and later drafted in CAD. this was simply to try to understand how on earth E+ was structuring the systems ond plants. In fact it was done on a tricky project - a glass hospital building with a double skin in the desert that had a very innovative HVAC system. (all credit to my colleague David Scheer - I just watched him do it :-)

Loopdiagram came from automating this process. @jamiebull1 claims that it works on most example files. I am shocked to hear that it actually works that well.

Hvacbuilder came from the knowledge extracted from loopdiagram. (I sometimes think of hvacbuilder as loopbuilder)

Which brings up an interesting point:
To code loopdiagram, a lot of detective work has to poring through the example files and the documentation. What is missing is the documentation on how to make these connections.

I propose we take a start at writing this "missing manual"

### Missing manual
- Will be a cookbook on how to make connections when putting in the systems or plants into an idf file.
- I wish the E+ documentation had such a manual. (most of the E+ documentation is excellent)
- but we are were we are. We might have to put this manual on our own
- The advantage of having such a manual would be:
    - makes it more transparent for people who are coding eppy
    - no need to do any detective work with the example files. The results of the detective work will be embodied in the manual.
    - the manual may be useful to others
    - putting together this manual will show us if the loop building within E+ is coherent or is it just ad-hoc. 
    
Having said this, to keep things practical, I propose that the coding on loopbuilder and the composing of the "missing manual" happen in parallel. I think it will feed on each other.
Or I may be deluded and the manual may just be a pie in the sky

Which brings up a new idea that came up in discussion with Joe Huang, that I call loopsucker.

### Loopsucker
This is a function that can suck a loop from one file into another file.
- Say, you have an example file with a system that you want to use in your project
- You have an idf file of your building with geometry, but no systems.
- This function will suck the system from the example file and plug it into your file making connections to all your zones.

With the knowledge from loopdiagram, hvacbuilder(loopbuilder) and the "missing manual" this may actually be possible.

A clipping from the idd file says the following::

    Connector:Splitter,
      \min-fields 3
           \extensible:1 Just duplicate last field and comments (changing numbering, please)
           \memo Split one air/water stream into N outlet streams.  Branch names cannot be duplicated
           \memo within a single Splitter list.
       A1, \field Name
            \required-field
       A2, \field Inlet Branch Name
            \required-field
            \type object-list
            \object-list Branches
       A3, \field Outlet Branch 1 Name
            \begin-extensible
            \required-field
            \type object-list
            \object-list Branches
       A4, \field Outlet Branch 2 Name
            \type object-list
            \object-list Branches
       A5, \field Outlet Branch 3 Name
            \type object-list
            \object-list Branches
    
    
- note that `A3` says `begin-extensible`
- These are defined earlier in the IDD file as follows::


        !  \begin-extensible  Marks the first field at which the object accepts an extensible
        !                   field set.  A fixed number of fields from this marker define the
        !                   extensible field set, see the object code \extensible for
        !                   more information.


        !  \extensible:<#>  This object is dynamically extensible -- meaning, if you
        !           change the IDD appropriately (if the object has a simple list
        !           structure -- just add items to the list arguments (i.e. BRANCH
        !           LIST). These will be automatically redimensioned and used during
        !           the simulation. <#> should be entered by the developer to signify
        !           how many of the last fields are needed to be extended (and EnergyPlus
        !           will attempt to auto-extend the object).  The first field of the first
        !           instance of the extensible field set is marked with \begin-extensible.
        !
        !  \begin-extensible See previous item, marks beginning of extensible fields in
        !                   an object.
    
- As a result of `A3` being `extensible`, the following fields `A4, A5, A6 .... ` will have the same characteristics as `A3`. Meaning, they will will also be `required-field`.

Hi @rodriguez-lucas,

The problem in your code is that when `lock_target_callback` is called, the value of the target state is **already** set, i.e. the callback is called after set value sets the value. What roughly happens is:
- iOS updates the target state - triggers `set_value`
- `set_value` (1) sets the value (2) calls the callback and (3) sends the updated value back to iOS

The below works (I reproduced the problem with your previous code):
```python
class LOCK_STATE:
   OPEN = 0
   CLOSED = 1

class ElectronicLock(Accessory):
    category = Category.DOOR

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(ElectronicLock, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.current = self.get_service("LockMechanism").get_characteristic("LockCurrentState")
        self.current.set_value(LOCK_STATE.CLOSED)  # just some initial value.
        self.target = self.get_service("LockMechanism").get_characteristic("LockTargetState")
        self.target.set_value(LOCK_STATE.CLOSED)

    def set_state(self, value):
        self.current.set_value(value)
        #self.target.set_value(value, should_callback=False)  <<< you don't need this

    def lock_target_callback(self, value):
        if value == LOCK_STATE.OPEN:
            print("OPEN")
        else:
            print("CLOSED")
        self.set_state(value)

    def _set_services(self):
        super(ElectronicLock, self)._set_services()
        lm_service = loader.get_serv_loader().get("LockMechanism")
        self.add_service(lm_service)
        lm_service.get_characteristic("LockTargetState").setter_callback = self.lock_target_callback
```

yes, if you don't know which file is which it is of course not very user-friendly. Here are some thoughts I had regarding this:

1 - Ideally, the file name could be encrypted with the same passphrase as the file and thus be decrypted just as easy. This, unfortunately, doesn't work as the encryption creates lots of characters you usually don't want in file names (e.g. '/') and also would make the file name length unusable (many cloud storage providers limit the file name length). So, unless there is a robust encryption algorithm that guarantees these requirements, it's probably not going to happen.

2 - Use some random UUID (e.g. uuid.uuid4() in python) to generate new filenames. You need to find a way to map these to the original file name. Right now I just store the mapping of each uuid to the original file name in a separate file. Of course this means you will need the second file to figure out which file is which. You could then just encrypt this (very small) file with the same passcode for each encrypted file or create a general "database" file containing all mappings for a directory.

To be sure you won't lose all your file names, even if you misplace this mapping file, I wrap all files inside a tar ball (uncompressed) before encryption. This way I just have to unpack the archive and don't have to rename any files.

For encryption it basically looks like this (tarfile then crypto):
SecretFileName.pdf -> 1234-5678-abcd.tar -> 1234-5678-abcd.tar.crypt
This way the file name (as well as creation time, etc.) of the original has never been changed and will be restored during decryption and unpacking, which is great if you want to keep your image creation dates.

One last note - Boxcryptor supports file name encryption, but I have no idea how they implemented it.

Thanks. Any suggestions for item 2: a way to generate consistent wmf images with dimensions that match other tools?

Btw, here is a test program that I use for debugging image issues. It is basically the image handling code extracted from the module. The first step would be to figure out where the dimensions/dpi are in the WMF file format and then second step write a new `_process_wmf()` method that prints them out from different sample images.

```python
from warnings import warn
from struct import unpack
import os
import sys


class MyClass:

    def __init__(self):
        self.image_types = {}
        self.images = []

    def _get_image_properties(self, filename, image_data=False):
        # Extract dimension information from the image file.
        height = 0
        width = 0
        x_dpi = 96
        y_dpi = 96

        if not image_data:
            # Open the image file and read in the data.
            fh = open(filename, "rb")
            data = fh.read()
        else:
            # Read the image data from the user supplied byte stream.
            data = image_data.getvalue()

        # Get the image filename without the path.
        image_name = os.path.basename(filename)

        # Look for some common image file markers.
        marker1 = (unpack('3s', data[1:4]))[0]
        marker2 = (unpack('>H', data[:2]))[0]
        marker3 = (unpack('2s', data[:2]))[0]

        if sys.version_info < (2, 6, 0):
            # Python 2.5/Jython.
            png_marker = 'PNG'
            bmp_marker = 'BM'
        else:
            # Eval the binary literals for Python 2.5/Jython compatibility.
            png_marker = eval("b'PNG'")
            bmp_marker = eval("b'BM'")

        if marker1 == png_marker:
            self.image_types['png'] = 1
            (image_type, width, height, x_dpi, y_dpi) = self._process_png(data)

        elif marker2 == 0xFFD8:
            self.image_types['jpeg'] = 1
            (image_type, width, height, x_dpi, y_dpi) = self._process_jpg(data)

        elif marker3 == bmp_marker:
            self.image_types['bmp'] = 1
            (image_type, width, height) = self._process_bmp(data)

        else:
            raise Exception("%s: Unknown or unsupported image file format."
                            % filename)

        # Check that we found the required data.
        if not height or not width:
            raise Exception("%s: no size data found in image file." % filename)

        # Store image data to copy it into file container.
        self.images.append([filename, image_type, image_data])

        if not image_data:
            fh.close()

        return image_type, width, height, image_name, x_dpi, y_dpi

    def _process_png(self, data):
        # Extract width and height information from a PNG file.
        offset = 8
        data_length = len(data)
        end_marker = False
        width = 0
        height = 0
        x_dpi = 96
        y_dpi = 96

        # Look for numbers rather than strings for Python 2.6/3 compatibility.
        marker_ihdr = 0x49484452  # IHDR
        marker_phys = 0x70485973  # pHYs
        marker_iend = 0X49454E44  # IEND

        # Search through the image data to read the height and width in the
        # IHDR element. Also read the DPI in the pHYs element.
        while not end_marker and offset < data_length:

            length = (unpack('>I', data[offset + 0:offset + 4]))[0]
            marker = (unpack('>I', data[offset + 4:offset + 8]))[0]

            # Read the image dimensions.
            if marker == marker_ihdr:
                width = (unpack('>I', data[offset + 8:offset + 12]))[0]
                height = (unpack('>I', data[offset + 12:offset + 16]))[0]

            # Read the image DPI.
            if marker == marker_phys:
                x_density = (unpack('>I', data[offset + 8:offset + 12]))[0]
                y_density = (unpack('>I', data[offset + 12:offset + 16]))[0]
                units = (unpack('b', data[offset + 16:offset + 17]))[0]

                if units == 1:
                    x_dpi = x_density * 0.0254
                    y_dpi = y_density * 0.0254

            if marker == marker_iend:
                end_marker = True
                continue

            offset = offset + length + 12

        return 'png', width, height, x_dpi, y_dpi

    def _process_jpg(self, data):
        # Extract width and height information from a JPEG file.
        offset = 2
        data_length = len(data)
        end_marker = False
        width = 0
        height = 0
        x_dpi = 96
        y_dpi = 96

        # Search through the image data to read the height and width in the
        # 0xFFC0/C2 element. Also read the DPI in the 0xFFE0 element.
        while not end_marker and offset < data_length:

            marker = (unpack('>H', data[offset + 0:offset + 2]))[0]
            length = (unpack('>H', data[offset + 2:offset + 4]))[0]

            # Read the image dimensions.
            if ((marker & 0xFFF0) == 0xFFC0
                    and marker != 0xFFC4
                    and marker != 0xFFC8
                    and marker != 0xFFCC):
                height = (unpack('>H', data[offset + 5:offset + 7]))[0]
                width = (unpack('>H', data[offset + 7:offset + 9]))[0]
                print "Marker: %04X, %d, %d" % (marker, height, width)

            # Read the image DPI.
            if marker == 0xFFE0:
                units = (unpack('b', data[offset + 11:offset + 12]))[0]
                x_density = (unpack('>H', data[offset + 12:offset + 14]))[0]
                y_density = (unpack('>H', data[offset + 14:offset + 16]))[0]

                if units == 1:
                    x_dpi = x_density
                    y_dpi = y_density

                if units == 2:
                    x_dpi = x_density * 2.54
                    y_dpi = y_density * 2.54

            if marker == 0xFFDA:
                end_marker = True
                continue

            offset = offset + length + 2

        return 'jpeg', width, height, x_dpi, y_dpi

    def _process_bmp(self, data):
        # Extract width and height information from a BMP file.
        width = (unpack('<L', data[18:22]))[0]
        height = (unpack('<L', data[22:26]))[0]
        return 'bmp', width, height


if __name__ == '__main__':
    x = MyClass()
    print "================================="
    print x._get_image_properties('some_test_image.jpg')
    print "================================="

I'm posting this code here because I've gotten it all on-line and there's some useful stuff distilled down into one bare bones dialog. Perhaps it's useful to others. 

import sys
from PyQt5.QtWidgets import QWidget, QApplication
from PyQt5.QtCore import QObject, QThread, pyqtSignal


import xlsxwriter

class TimerThread(QObject):

    quitSignal = pyqtSignal(object)

    def __init__(self):
        QThread.__init__(self)
        
    def run(self):
        self.workBook = xlsxwriter.Workbook("sheetNameIssue.xlsx")
        self.scopeSheet = self.workBook.add_worksheet("Voltage Regulator")
        self.summarySheet = self.workBook.add_worksheet("Summary")

        self.summarySheet.write(1, 0, 0)
        self.summarySheet.write(2, 0, 1)
        self.summarySheet.write(3, 0, 2)
        self.summarySheet.write(4, 0, 3)
        self.summarySheet.write(5, 0, 4)

        self.summarySheet.write(1, 1, 0)
        self.summarySheet.write(2, 1, 1)
        self.summarySheet.write(3, 1, 2)
        self.summarySheet.write(4, 1, 3)
        self.summarySheet.write(5, 1, 4)
                        
        self.stopTimer()



    def stopTimer(self):
        resultsChart = self.workBook.add_chart({'type': 'scatter'})
        resultsChart.add_series({'categories': "=Summary!$A$2:$A$5",'values':"=Summary!$B$2:$B$5"})

        # Add a chart title and some axis labels.
        resultsChart.set_title ({'name': 'Slammer Frequency Sweep Results'})
        resultsChart.set_x_axis({'name': 'Slammer Frequency'})
        resultsChart.set_y_axis({'name': 'Peak to Peak Voltage'})

        # Set an Excel chart style.
        resultsChart.set_style(13)

        # Insert the chart into the worksheet (with an offset).
        self.summarySheet.insert_chart('D3', resultsChart)

        self.workBook.close()
        self.quitSignal.emit(self.quitSignal)


class MainWindow(QWidget):
    def __init__(self):
        super(MainWindow, self).__init__()
        self.startThread()
        self.show()

    def startThread(self):
        self.work = TimerThread() 
        self.thread = QThread()
        self.work.quitSignal.connect(self.quitLoop)

        self.work.moveToThread(self.thread)
        self.thread.started.connect(self.work.run)
        self.thread.start()

    def quitLoop(self):
                
        self.thread.quit()
        print("Got quit signal")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    
    app = QApplication(sys.argv)
    ex = MainWindow()
    sys.exit(app.exec_())

Thanks for opening the PR! Before doing too much more work I would emphasize that you should clarify which functions you would like to add and why those ones are worth adding. As I mentioned before I would like to avoid essentially doubling the API surface without good reason.

For example, `reduce_by_key` does not need this since it should be called through something like `reduce_by_key(lambda x, y: x + y)` so its already unpacked as a result of the signature for the input function being known (precisely, given a sequence of items `List[A]` the function should have signature `(A, A) -> A`)

There are others such as `max_by` et al where a starred version really isn't necessary since the most common use case is to pick out one value for which a simple index or attribute call is fine. Starmap is useful for `map` since its common to need access to multiple elements of the tuple.

With regards to implementation I don't like the automated approach since it doesn't do things such as docstrings and breaks editor autocomplete/linters, and would much prefer defining them inline. Its also important that the PR passes all of the automated Travis CI tests.

I thought it might also be good to mention a trick that I frequently use. When I have structured data as a tuple I'll often define a `named tuple` class then do something like `seq(stuff).smap(MyNamedTuple)...` which gives attribute like access for everything else. It also has a benefit that if you want to view it in the terminal/jupyter that it displays formatted as a table. For the most part this handles the cases where I would need to have stars in many places without sacrificing readability

So I went through the code this afternoon. The confusion comes from my poorly written README. I even spent an hour scratching my head. I have now updated the code; placing some error checking, comments and sensible defaults.

I'll explain the example you posted a little better:

```
from ratelimit import rate_limited
import datetime

@rate_limited(2)
def rate_limited_function():
    print("function called at: {}".format(datetime.datetime.now()))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    while True:
        rate_limited_function()
```

So to clarify, the arguments of the decorator are to be read as run the `rate_limited_function` function at most twice per second. You may also pass in a second argument that can change the time window.

```
from ratelimit import rate_limited
import datetime

@rate_limited(8, 2.5)
def rate_limited_function():
    print("function called at: {}".format(datetime.datetime.now()))
```

This therefore reads as run the `rate_limited_function` function at most six times every 2.5 seconds. If you ran the method on every iteration of an infinite loop you should see the method being called every ~0.3125 seconds.

I hope this makes sense.

Huh. Ok, so there are two different kinds of Wormhole objects: `_DeferredWormhole` (which has methods like `.get_code()` that return a Deferred), and `_DelegatedWormhole` (which has no such methods, instead it takes a delegate object at creation time and invokes methods like `.wormhole_got_code(code)` on the delegate). The main entry point, `from wormhole import wormhole; w=wormhole(args..)` can return either, depending upon whether you provide a delegate or not. At present, both flavors declare themselves as implementers of `IWormhole`, but `IWormhole` itself is hidden in `wormhole._interfaces`.

For internal type-checking purposes (to make sure I don't swap the arguments around), `IWormhole` really refers to an entirely different set of methods, which *are* provided by both flavors: things like `.got_code(code)`, which is called by one of the internal child state-machine objects. By which I mean that those internal child state-machine objects all `assert IWormhole.providedBy(w)` on the `w` argument they're given, and then they invoke `self._w.got_code(code)` and such.

I can't put the `_DeferredWormhole` methods on `IWormhole`, since they aren't provided by `_DelegatedWormhole`. I could put the internal methods on `IWormhole`, but that's not something that should be exposed to anyone else. The canonical docs on what a Wormhole object does is `docs/api.md`.

You've been using Interfaces for longer than me.. any guidance as to what I should do with `IWormhole`?

Before merging:

1. Refactor test data into a class/module
2. Look into how boto3 handles default regions. All of ALC lets the default region be ``None`` and passes this through to boto. With boto3, there's a change to the underlying environment variable use. I found this out by running ``awslimitchecker -vv -u -S ElastiCache`` after 32416b7, and got vastly different results from the latest release. It turns out that the new boto3 code is connecting to ``us-west-2``. It appears that with boto2, if you set the ``AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`` and ``AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`` environment variables, it completely ignores ``~/.aws/credentials``. With boto3, however, if you have a ``[default]`` section in ``~/.aws/credentials`` and then export the key env vars, it still uses the ``region`` from ``~/.aws/credentials``.

The client-side build integration proved to be the most complicated part of the system, and produced the largest amount of issues, questions and frustrations. It's a similar story for [react-rails](https://github.com/reactjs/react-rails). 

The approach taken by python-webpack's pretty crappy and I personally consider that project to be deprecated. It's much simpler to interact directly with external build tools. There are a couple of tools which take this approach and produce manifest files, which the python process can consume and introspect.

Owais has a bunch of examples up at his [django-webpack-loader](https://github.com/owais/django-webpack-loader) project

I've got a similar project: [python-webpack-manifest](https://github.com/markfinger/python-webpack-manifest). Given that it's a tiny codebase, I doubt I'll ever document it to the degree that Owais has.

Cheers,
Mark

This looks like a certificate issue with pmi.com. Your output says: `Certificate did not match expected hostname: pmi.com` and then `exchangelib.errors.AutoDiscoverFailed: All steps in the autodiscover protocol failed`.

So somehow your Python installation is not able to validate the certificate of pmi.com. exchangelib then continues with the autodiscover process until all other options are exhausted, and fails.

If you think you should be able to autodiscover pmi.com email addresses, then first get an autodiscover test report on https://testconnectivity.microsoft.com. If that works, then match the report against the steps exchangelib is taking and find you where we're doing things differently. You may want to install exchangelib from the master branch on GitHub - I just committed better debug output for the autodiscover process.

Is this issue still valid? Because when looking at the code I can see that in `getAllSystemVariables` you are actually using `SysVar.getAll()`, which fetches all variables in one request (+2 for login/logoff). I think this actually looks good :)

If you want to get rid of the 2 requests for authentication, you could also use the optional XML-API addon (`/addons/xmlapi/sysvarlist.cgi`). Since you already support that addon for resolving names this would not be an entirely new dependency.

This actually has 2 benefits:

- 1 HTTP request for getting all variables, no authentication
- You could also set string variables through the XML-API, which JSON-RPC does not allow

So supporting the XML-API as an alternative way for variable operations would actually have multiple benefits.

One common use case is to ask "Hey, is this URL-looking thing (probably) a valid URL?" 

There are a lot of ways to potentially write that as code, but it often largely boils down to something like...

        if tldextract(candidate).registered_domain or othercode.is_ipv4(candidate):

the idea is to just consolidate it a bit:

        extracted = tldextract(candidate)
        if extracted.registered_domain or extracted.ipv4:

it's not too hard to do the ipv4 stuff in othercode, but doing it in tldextract keeps others from reimplementing things and avoids another parsing of a full URL (if that is the input).  in other words, if `candidate` is a url and not a host, it's considerably cheaper (and more convenient) to have the ipv4 status accessible.

This was kind of bug.
`setup.py` checking parent directory's `rpm.pc` from `pkgconfig` command in `setup.py`.
https://github.com/rpm-software-management/rpm/blob/rpm-4.14.x/python/setup.py.in#L25

But in `rpm-py-installer`'s install process, there is no `rpm.pc` in the parent directory.

So, the solution is creating from `rpm.pc.in` to `rpm.pc` manually in the install process.

So, `WebSocketHandler` from `gevent-websocket` library tries to upgrade any kind of connection into WS connection.

This is a valid logic, since a web server listens for all incoming connections on a single socket. Hence, all connections are passed through same logic: [handler checks whether connection is a websocket connection or not](https://bitbucket.org/noppo/gevent-websocket/src/e366791ba8793ff7df8e0a80939092f77fe4667b/geventwebsocket/handler.py?at=0.9.5&fileviewer=file-view-default#handler.py-110). And there are vivid messages for that:

```python

if self.environ.get('REQUEST_METHOD', '') != 'GET':
    # This is not a websocket request, so we must not handle it
    self.logger.debug('Can only upgrade connection if using GET method.')
    return

if upgrade == 'websocket':
    ...
else:
    # This is not a websocket request, so we must not handle it
    return
```

So, your messages

> Initializing WebSocket
> Sep 25 04:12:32 surcle app/web.1: Validating WebSocket request
> Sep 25 04:12:32 surcle app/web.1: Can only upgrade connection if using GET method. 

are not errors, they are just debug messages. Nothing goes wrong.

I agree that they are confusing and the only thing you can do is to [open an issue in gevent-websocket](https://bitbucket.org/noppo/gevent-websocket/issues/new) and to ask to write more correct messages to logs.

Other thing you can do is set up a logging handler for a logger named `geventwebsocket.server` and ignore debug messages coming from it.

It's already possible what you want. See http://python-logstash-async.readthedocs.io/en/latest/config.html#options-for-configuring-the-log-formatter and in detail you need to set the optons `extra` and `extra_prefix`.

`extra` is a dictionary of additional message fields to be submitted to Logstash, e.g. your environment.
`extra_prefix` is the prefix or namespace into which the extra fields should be submitted. If you set this to None or the empty string, the additional fields will be submitted to the top level of the event, this is probably what you want.

Unfortunately, you cannot change these options with the ini configuration file due to the limited options available to the formatter section. This is a limitation of Python's logging framework.
I'd suggest to migrate your logging configuration to the dict configuration which is way more flexible.
Examples can be found in the docs: http://python-logstash-async.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html.

What version of Django are you using? `get_all_related_objects` is an internal Django API that has changed several times. Actually, it is deprecated in 1.9 and will be removed soon.
Django transition documentation recommends using this instead:

```python
[
    f for f in MyModel._meta.get_fields()
    if (f.one_to_many or f.one_to_one) and f.auto_created
]
```

It could help with your issue as, normally, all fields should be in `get_fields`, and from that point is should be easy to narrow down which fields pass and fail the filter and why.

> the .pth installed with your python package seems to be ignored.

According to [the docs](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/site.html), a .pth file must exist in one of four directories, so you wouldn't expect it to be honored if it's only in PYTHONPATH.

The problem isn't with this package, but with the packaging tool you're using and the way you're expecting it to work.

`pip` by design expects all packages to be installed in a site-packages and relies on the -nspkg.pth files to satisfy the namespace packages (such as `backports` is).

Your best bet is that if you're using a virtual environment (as it appears you are in `env`) is just invoke Python from there.

```
env/bin/python2.7 -c "import backports"
```

Or even better, use Python 3.3 or later, which I believe has support for namespace packages without requiring the `__init__.py` or `-nspkg.pth` files.

```python
import mxnet as mx
import sys
import os
import scipy
import numpy as np
from mxnet.test_utils import rand_ndarray, assert_almost_equal
import time

def measure_cost(repeat, a, b, c, d, e, out=None):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(mx.nd.sparse.add_n(a, b, c, d, e, out=out))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def measure_fallback(repeat, a):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(a.tostype('default'))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def main():
    shape = (1000000, 128)
    dns = np.random.uniform(size=shape)
    context = mx.gpu(0)
    # context = mx.cpu()
    mx_dns1 = mx.nd.array(dns, ctx=context)
    mx_dns2 = mx.nd.array(dns, ctx=context)
    mx_dns3 = mx.nd.array(dns, ctx=context)
    for density in [0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001]:
        mx_csr = rand_ndarray(shape=shape, stype='csr', density=density).as_in_context(context)
        mx_csr_dns = mx_csr.tostype('default')
        mx_rsp = rand_ndarray(shape=shape, stype='row_sparse', density=density).as_in_context(context)
        mx_rsp_dns = mx_rsp.tostype('default')
        sparse_cost = 0.0
        dns_cost = 0.0
        mx.nd.waitall()
        #warmup
        check = mx.nd.sparse.add_n(mx_dns1, mx_csr, mx_rsp, mx_dns2, mx_dns3)
        dns1 = dns + mx_csr_dns.asnumpy() + mx_rsp_dns.asnumpy() + dns + dns
        assert_almost_equal(check.asnumpy(), dns1, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
        mx.nd.waitall()
        for i in range(20):
            sparse_cost += measure_cost(5, mx_dns1, mx_csr, mx_dns2, mx_rsp, mx_dns3)
            dns_cost += measure_cost(5, mx_dns1, mx_csr_dns, mx_dns2, mx_rsp_dns, mx_dns3)
        print("%.2f %%" % (density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Hey @dpopowich, we seem to be having a misunderstanding here. You need to distinguish between the spec (i.e. how data is being sent over the wire) and what you get if you use bravado-core to unmarshal objects for you.

The design decision was made quite some time ago to make sure all attributes should be present, whether they were sent or not. If they were not sent then the value would be `None`. This was fine since you weren't allowed to send attributes with the value of `None` anyway - that's how you distinguish them.

I've now repeatedly told you how to implement this. Have you ever actually tried it out? If an attribute has the value `None` then _it was not sent by the other side_. There is no other way in which a property can be `None` (unless you use the nonstandard `x-nullable` extension). Please try it out - I don't see any blockers to implementing the behavior you're looking for.

Facebook's Watchman is a service that is **not** installed through `npm`.  https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/install.html has installation instructions.

There is an `npm` module called `watchman` that is completely unrelated and that unfortunately has a name collision and causes confusion.  You will need to uninstall that package if you want to use the Facebook Watchman service.

If you want to use nodejs to talk to the Facebook Watchman service, you can use `npm` to install `fb-watchman`.

def flatten(self, obj, data):
        data["name"] = obj.name
        data["age"] = obj.age

See https://github.com/spotify/annoy/blob/master/src/annoylib.h#L399 for how memory is used while adding items. It isn't until you call `save` that the index is written to disk. Then later if you call `load` it will perform an mmap. It would make more sense to support mmap during insertion too. Problem is afaik mmap doesn't support resizing, but you could probably just write to the file pointer, flush, then munmap/mmap again (not sure how fast it would be). This way you would always use the file system for persistance, and the kernel will use the page cache to fit as much as possible in RAM. With an SSD this would be pretty reasonable

I was going to point out the inconsistency in the [boto3 documentation](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.Client.copy_snapshot) (DestinationRegion is described in the Parameters section, but not used in the Request Syntax section)... but now that I read this...

If I read correctly, you're saying the ec2 client.copy_snapshot does not use the DestinationRegion at all, because it uses the "current" region (from the client's session) as the destination. And, indeed, it **requires** the SourceRegion argument (feels like we could call this a "pull" model).

If I'm writing a script to run, for instance, on my machine, I can establish a session with the source region, [look among the snapshots](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.Paginator.DescribeSnapshots) to find one I wish to copy (grab its id), then establish a separate/new session with the destination region, and use the ec2 client copy_snapshot to perform the copy.
Feels a bit clunky, but ...hmm.. ok..

But if I am a Lambda?
If I'm a Lambda running _in the source region_, then it's easy to look at the snapshots available to find the one I want to copy, but then I have to make a separate session with the destination region, and use that client's copy_snapshot method. Right?

'Cause if my Lambda was running _in the destination region_ (in which case the client on which I call copy_snapshot can use the implicit current region), I don't think I have a way to get the list of available snapshots from the source region ([no filter in DescribeSnapshots talks about regions](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.Paginator.DescribeSnapshots))... except by opening a separate session with the source region and using a client from that session ...

It does feel clunky, doesn't it?

`GetBucketLifecycle` (`get_bucket_lifecycle`) is deprecated in favor of `GetBucketLifecycleConfiguration` (`get_bucket_lifecycle_configuration`).

When this feature was initially shipped by S3, it supported only a single rule. They since added the ability to specify multiple rules, which meant that we needed to support the version that provided only a single value as a dict for backwards compatibility, but also support a list of dicts. This meant adding a new operation and marking the old form as deprecated.

Just some background on why I created this change request.

I noticed that icalendar was one of _very few_ packages that included tests installed to my virtualenv. I prefer to keep dependencies and dead code to a minimum. Just less code surface area to consider.

I don't want other packages' tests available to any part of my production system. Should some module or test discovery system wander into icalendar's tests, I prefer they never be executed. Not including them is the best way to ensure this.

In my opinion, tests are a development tool, not a production tool. Tests can still be run either through the source distribution or a git clone.

Hi,

The first thing that comes to my mind is that GitPython is not thread-save at all. Therefore you must not share anything related to and including the `Repo` object, unless all access is behind a mutex. Thus, each thread needs at least its own `Repo` instance. Once that is done, you might actually be hindered by the GIL, which will only be released if a thread is waiting for IO. Doing Multithreading in Python is usually prevented by it, and `MultiProcessing` should be used instead.

The easiest way to (possibly) gain speed in a single thread is to [initialize the `Repo` instance with a `GitCmdObjectDB`](http://gitpython.readthedocs.org/en/stable/tutorial.html#gitcmdobjectdb).

I don't think the issue you experience is in any way related to *smmap*.

Good Luck - please close the issue if you see my point.

Thanks for posting! I can reproduce the issue as well.
The fix is to not quote the string, so `--match="001.005"` becomes `--match=001.005`.

Reason for this is that `GitPython` does not use a shell to execute git, but calls it 'directly'. The quotes are usually processed by your shell and are not visible to `git`.
However, the python code presented here literally tries to find a tag that matches `"001.005"`, which does not exist.

@philfreo Pipenv works quite differently than pip in concept, however. `pipenv install` does a lot more than `pip install`—it also adds the package into Pipfile, and updates Pipfile.lock. The verb `install` is also used in `pipenv install` (no arguments), which means “updates Pipfile.lock from Pipfile, and installs all packages in Pipfile.lock”, which is quite different from `pipenv install <package>`. `install` is a traditionally more used verb, but `add` is picking up popularity recently to disambiguous the action from the sterotypical “install” action, even in languages with well-established manifest-lock terminology (e.g. Yarn vs NPM).

I would even say you solidified the reasoning to favour `add` by misidentifying the naming implication :p

https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/1450 should fix the PyParsing ones.

setuptools calls `make_distribution` of distutils, in which `base_dir` shall be modified to prefix the `temproot`. However, we cannot simply copy the code from distutils as an override, because the function changed in an incompatible way in python 3.4 (4 years ago)

https://github.com/python/cpython/blame/master/Lib/distutils/command/sdist.py#L481

Python 2.x the function hasn't changed for 8 years, so it's probably ok to override:

https://github.com/python/cpython/blame/2.7/Lib/distutils/command/sdist.py#L443

This PR as presented doesn't depend on OrderedDict unless the suggested adaptation is made, in which case, dropping Python 2.6 does help with that. I'm also willing to accept a vendored library for ordered dict to add that support with Python 2.6 support.

But I did realize later there's another issue with relying on the `namespace_packages` declaration in the call to `setup`, and that's that the use of `namespace_packages` is [discouraged](https://python-packaging-user-guide.readthedocs.io/guides/packaging-namespace-packages/#pkg-resources-style-namespace-packages).

This all leads me to believe that this explicit though sometimes duplicative declaration indicated in this PR as is may be the the best option.

On further investigation it turns out that `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` is a threading library based module and does not internally use `asyncio`.

Also, I learned a few things about asyncio :

1. Using async calls does not make network/disk calls asynchronous automatically - using a library that does these things asynchronously from the ground-up is required. This means network/disk inside the event loop actually block the event loop unless we are using libraries like [aiohttp](https://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) and aiofiles (appears to be a newish library).
2. Due to the above, use of `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` is required to fire-off such blocking calls in a separate thread to make them _appear_ asynchronous, and to integrate nicely with asyncio. This means that there will be thread-switching inside the executor - this was something we wanted to avoid in the first place.

Due to these problems it would not be a clean solution for py3. It would be better to reconsider this option in the future and see if a cleaner solution appears.

An interesting point is that a high-quality boto based async s3 library is [here](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore).

So, IMO we should go ahead with merging this PR.

The exception handling in the current PR is not correct - the exception inside a task needs to be rethrown in the main thread, instead of being just printed and otherwise ignored. It appears that `multiprocessing.dummy.Pool` would work in both py3 and py2 and it re-raises exceptions from threads in the calling thread. If there are no other issues, we should go back to that simple solution.

I'm trying to distill this issue to its base. You'd like some mode of operation in which any "new" consumer group (no committed offsets) starts from the latest *available* offset, and any "existing" consumer group (with >= 1 committed offset) starts from its latest *committed* offset. That is, some single combination of settings that behaves in this way. Is this an accurate assessment?

Assuming it is, and thinking about the existing options: `reset_offset_on_start` being `True` makes a new consumer group start at the latest available offset, and an existing consumer do the same - not what you're looking for. This option being `False` makes a new consumer group start at offset `0` as you've described, and makes an existing group start from its latest committed offset - also not what you're looking for.

I mean it is necessary for us to use `python setup.py test --coverage` so we can also get our cython code compiled and have it tested as well.

For the record, the problem was introduced by #185, which attempts to perform some voodoo to shim Cython support into the build_ext command *late* in the setup process, after `setup_requires` packages have already been processed.  That way there can be a functional build_ext command even if Cython is not available.  But if Cython is then installed via `setup_requires`, support for Cython's version of `build_ext` can be shimmed in at the last second.

This was tricky to get right though, and the existing implementation has some flaws.  In particular it doesn't end up implementing the correct class hierarchy, such that `Cython.Distutils.build_ext.initialize_options` is never run, leading to that attribute error.

I've reworked this so that if/when Cython becomes available, the `build_ext` command supplied by astropy-helpers actually gets its `__bases__` updated in-place.  This is of course usually discouraged, but it can be done if you're careful.

@soupault that's not how preserve range was defined. Indeed, it's a lot fuzzier than this. `preserve_range` simply means that no rescaling will take place, regardless of dtype conversions. So if the algorithm requires a 16-bit image, and you give it a uint8 image, it will return a uint16 image in 0-255, and this might cause issues later on in your pipeline, for example when saving and displaying the image, which will now look very dark. (Which is why `preserve_range` always defaults to `False`). More commonly, it means that your uint8 image will be converted to a float image in 0-255 before further processing in an algorithm that depends on floating point values. But, the output range is in no way guaranteed. For example, when using an algorithm that depends on cubic interpolation (such as warp, coincidentally), you might get values higher than 255 and lower than 0.

When `preserve_range=False`, uint8 (range 0-255) will get rescaled to [0, 1] floats, [0-65335] uint16s, and so on. int8 will get rescaled to [-1, 1] floats. etc. And, clipping will take place to ensure outputs are in the predefined range for that dtype.

I guess this needs to be expanded upon in the documentation. I agree with @dvolgyes that there are many docstrings where this isn't very clear at all. At the very least, we should link to http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/data_types.html wherever `preserve_range` is used.

+5. What about an `invenio/ext/msgpack` module which introduces some commonly used types?

+3. 10y was not meant to be a standard. It was more meant to be like "do not alter the format on every update". About the update procedure: As far as I know we only use msgpack for the broker and celery results (not for the session storage, because that one uses pickle which is assumed to be fine in that case). If we alter the format, the admin has to do the following:
 1. block new incoming broker messages (maintenance mode starts here)
 2. flush entire msg queue
 3. wait until all messages and their results are processed
 4. update invenio (format changes at this point)
 5. unlock broker (maintenance mode ends here)

Alternative: Introduce new format at new slot, keep the deserializer for the old format alive and run serializer@new and deserializer@new+old for one/some version(s). Put a clear note into the update to tell the admin what happens when he skips specific versions.

In general we should find a way to flush the queues, so the first option would be a very useful admin tool anyway. Maybe this is already possible? (cc @lnielsen)

Well, there is a problem in getting rid of the `SetDefaultIndex` bit.  You need this to make `'index': {'type': 'Random'}` work properly, since it needs to set the maximum value for the random integer.  I think you only want to skip that line if there is no `index` at all in the config parameters:
```
if 'index' in config:
    galsim.config.SetDefaultIndex(config, cosmos_cat.getNObjects())
```

As for the mismatch between the config and python versions, I predict that you will find them to be in sync when you run `galsim demo11.yaml image.nproc=1`.

The problem here is that when running with multiple processes, the rng gets serialized when communicating between the processes.  The input objects all live in the main process (so we don't have nproc copies of the cosmos catalog in memory).  When you call `selectRandomIndices`, the rng you give it as a kwarg gets serialized and then reconstituted on the other side.  This means the one in the child process building the galaxy doesn't actually change.

See `input_powerspectrum` for another case of this.  For that case, the number of random calls was deterministic, so I added a function `nRandCallsForBuildGrid` that returned the number of values to `discard` in the child process.  In your cases, `selectRandomIndices` may call the rng a variable number of times, so the same solution won't work.

You have two options I think.  
1. Have `selectRandomIndices` (optionally) also return the number of times the random number generator was called.  Then if you're using a proxy (cf. config/input_powerspectrum.py for how to check this) discard that many from the rng you have there.  

2. Make `selectRandomIndices` a `staticmethod` similar to `_makeSingleGalaxy`.  Then it will be run from the current process and the rng will be updated properly.  You can't use any attributes in that case, but you might be able to do everything you need via method calls, similar to what `_makeSingleGalaxy` does.

>This is (typically) based on the galaxy flux, which isn't necessarily a trivial quantity that would be known in advance.

Right.  Forgot about those pesky galaxies...

>However, I don't think we want the rng for the boiling to be associated with the object anyway. 

Agreed.  I meant just meant the rng passed to `shoot` (say, to randomly sample the pupil plane).  The rng that boils the atmosphere is still separate.  As you say, though, n_photons is also a problem.

I guess I'll implement things the slow way here, and leave efficient looping for a future issue.  In principle, I think it should still be possible to loop efficiently, just not in a way that's easily compatible with the current API.  We'd have to do something like delay all work until the Image array is accessed, which sounds tricky...

Some investigation of [Gaussian random fields](https://github.com/kadrlica/sandbox/blob/master/notebooks/GaussianRandomFields.ipynb). The basic idea is that eventually we will want to adjust the height field of the CCD surface layers to adjust the physical scale of the fringing.

The fringing obviously depends on wavelength, but we are going to need to decide how much the spatial pattern and amplitude of fringing can be separated from the other surface-layer operations (conversion depth and incident angle). In reality they are all bundled up, but we may need to see how far we can get assuming they are separable.

I think the former already fits in well with the current paradigm of returning a dict.  Just the pupil plane image would be different for different bands.  If there is an efficiency consideration, this could be enabled only if `high_accuracy=True`, but I doubt there is, so I'd just have it always use the right pupil plane image for each band whenever `approx_struts=False`.

The variation across the focal plane would normally require the user to call this function repeatedly -- once for each location on the focal plane where the PSF is requested.  For this, I suspect we want to add a new kwarg, `image_pos`, which would normally be just a `PositionD`, and SCAs would just be a single number.  But in general, the user could provide a list for each of them.  (SCAs is currently either a list of a single integer.)  The default `image_pos` would be the center of the image (2044, 2044) if the user doesn't specify something else.

Thanks for being part of the Font Awesome Community.

A generic icon for a compressed archive is already here: https://fontawesome.com/icons/file-archive?style=solid

At the moment, I'm going to close this request because:
- You can use the SVG layering feature to implement file extensions (see example);
- You can stack text with icons if you are using CSS or FA 4.7;
- At the basic icon size of 16px, a three letter extension will be too large compared to the icon itself;
- I would like to avoid requests for `.HTML`, `.CSS`, `.JS`, `.SVG`, `.DAT`, `.PNG`, etc.

Example (SVG): https://jsfiddle.net/tagliala/vLae8ted/7/

Docs (SVG): https://fontawesome.com/how-to-use/svg-with-js#layering
Blog post (CSS): http://blog.fontawesome.io/2014/05/19/stacking-text-and-icons/

```python
    def __call__(self, environ, start_response):
        """Process this response as WSGI application.

        :param environ: the WSGI environment.
        :param start_response: the response callable provided by the WSGI
                               server.
        :return: an application iterator
        """
        app_iter, status, headers = self.get_wsgi_response(environ)
        start_response(status, headers)
        return app_iter

Hi, the basic structure looks good. One question is that are you using any customized context provider in your application? 

The default context used by the X-Ray recorder store segments/subsegments on ThreadLocal. This is fine when a request/response cycle is tied to a thread. Asyncio doesn't operate on thread. It operates on asyncio.Task object.   

In case of aiohttp, each handler coroutine becomes one Task in the event loop. It is necessary to have a custom context that associates a segment to a task. That way it guarantees any subsegments (downstream calls to AWS or sql queries etc) generated from the handler can be attached to the correct segment. 

Please let me know if you have any extra changes in your application running on aiohttp or any findings on load testing.

hi @dwhjames, I wanted to revisit this. We recently merged a large refactor of the ABI implementation (https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/pull/926), which notably features an `ABI.serialize` interface that takes a type spec, which can be used to more correctly serialize arguments without having to guess purely based on the python type passed in.

It does not currently support fixed sized bytes well (https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/issues/952), which I understand was one of the main points of this PR, but it should not be a hard fix (we would also love help with this 😅 !)

It should support serializing external functions via `ABI.serialize('function', (address, selector))` (e.g `ABI.serialize('function', (0xfB6916095ca1df60bB79Ce92cE3Ea74c37c5d359, '\xaa\xbb\xcc\xdd'))`).

I think this should largely address the issues you were dealing with in a relatively clean way :) Do you have any other thoughts?

Once we fix #952 I think we should be able to close this PR.

I friend I showed this Github issue to suggested that I include a proposed draft of changes to the "Hacking on Manticore page.  Below is a draft of a replacement "Developer Installation" section for that page.  Note that this draft is likely incomplete, as I have not yet produced an entirely functional Manticore install and therefore am not able to include the step or prerequisites that I have not yet derived (such as through error messages).  For similar reasons, this draft might also include obsolete instructions that I cannot test for.  Lastly, there may be a more preferable way to have the needed Python packages installed than the pip3 commands I outline below (I myself used the Linux Mint repos to install the corresponding python3-* packages); alternatively, it may be better to have the installation script automatically install the listed packages (as is done for other prerequisite Python packages).


### Developer Installation

Manticore uses `cmake` during the build process; if you do not already have `cmake` installed on your system, you can install it using one of the below commands:

Ubuntu/Debian/Linux Mint/`apt`-based distros:
```
sudo apt-get install cmake
```

Fedora/CentOS/RHEL/`yum`-based distros:
```
sudo yum install cmake
```


Manticore also requires the Etherum package `solc`; you can run one of the command blocks below to add the Etherum repository to your system's list of repositories and then install the `solc` package:

Ubuntu/Debian/Linux Mint/`apt`-based distros:
```
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:ethereum/ethereum
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install solc
```

Fedora/CentOS/RHEL/`yum`-based distros:  Etherum does not currently have a `yum` based repository; you can instead [build `solc` from source](https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/v0.4.24/installing-solidity.html#building-from-source).


The Manticore installation script will install most of the Python packages that are required by Manticore; the below command will install Python packages that Manticore requires but are _not_ installed by the installation script:

```
pip3 install wheel pyelftools keystone z3-solver
```


For a dev install that includes dependencies for tests, run:

```
git clone https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore.git && cd manticore
pip3 install --no-binary keystone-engine -e .[dev]
```

Note: The above `pip3` command tries to install to the system install directory, which might fail with "Permission Denied". We recommend alternatively using a virtualenv or a user install (`pip3 install --no-binary keystone-engine --user -e .[dev]`; installs everything into ~/.local).

The -e flag to `pip3` will install the Manticore package in "editable" mode. This means you don't have to reinstall the package after making changes to the source.

You can run the tests with the commands below. The `nosetests3` utility is installed automatically by the prior `pip3` command and can be used to conveniently run tests.

```
cd manticore
# all tests
nosetests3
# just one file
nosetests3 tests/test_armv7cpu.py
# just one test class
nosetests3 tests/test_armv7cpu.py:Armv7CpuInstructions
# just one test
nosetests3 tests/test_armv7cpu.py:Armv7CpuInstructions.test_mov_imm_min
```

Alternatively, you can invoke tests via `python3 -m nose`.

Moreover, you can invoke multiprocess test invocation via the --processes flag. Note, however, that several tests (e.g., tests/test_memdumps.py) require longer execution times, thus you need to specify the appropriate timeout period via the --process-timeout flag. E.g.,

```
nosetests3 --processes=8 --process-timeout=120 tests/test_binaries.py
```

Hi @rbcarson, @behackett,

To summarize above:

1) Current version of requests_kerberos implements something non-standard. It sends unwrapped Kerberos token. For example, Apache HTTP Client for java does the same, but uses "Kerberos" instead of "Negotiate" in the HTTP header. It looks like fortunately GSSAPI/SSPI can somehow distinguish Kerberos and SPNEGO tokens when you pass content of "Negotiate" to them (tested with Java implementation of GSSAPI by passing both mechanisms to GSSAPI on server side)

2) Some sort of negotiation is possible if your server accepts multiple authentication schemes by sending something like this: WWW-Authenticate: Kerberos, NTLM, Basic realm="REALM NAME" - and you add multiple handlers on client side:
    session.auth = SomeDispatcherBasedOnValueOfWWWAuthenticate(
       requests_kerberos.HTTPKerberosAuth(mutual_authentication=requests_kerberos.OPTIONAL),
       requests_ntlm.HttpNtlmAuth('domain\\username','password'),
       ,,,
   )
At least in Apache HTTP Client this is done this way.

3) Negotiation in terms of https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4559 is the goal of this pull request.
But actual authentication mechanisms available for negotiation depend in GSSAPI on Linux, and SSPI on Windows.

I've been thinking some more about this. On a *nix machine you login locally, then call kinit to get a TGT. Starting with krb5 1.10 you can take advantage of [credential cache collections](http://k5wiki.kerberos.org/wiki/Projects/Credential_cache_collection_improvements) with the addition of kswitch and improvements to klist to get TGTs for multiple principals. gss_acquire_cred can find the correct credentials in the correct cache for the principal you specify.

On Windows the situation is very different. First, there is no kswitch, and klist only lists existing tickets or tgts. It doesn't allow you to get new tickets. The documentation for pszPrincipal appears to be wrong. How it is supposed to work is a mystery. [Comments here](http://www.kerberos.org/software/samples/gsskrb5/gsskrb5-1011/krb5/krb5cred.c) claim that it is ignored in the Kerberos SSP. [This post](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/microsoft.public.platformsdk.security/Xlo23EMiH9U) from back in 2001 claims it must be NULL.

All that said, you can still use an alternative principal. Just split principal at '@' passing the first part to the user parameter of winkerberos.authGSSClientInit and the second part to the domain parameter. For this to work in all situations you'll likely also have to expose the password parameter. Since my dev VMs aren't joined to a kerberos realm and therefore I can't use single sign on, I've tested this extensively and it works well. See [PyMongo's integration](https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-python-driver/blob/master/pymongo/auth.py#L293-L303) as an example.

Yeah, it's a real problem. It's why, ultimately, most "managed-memory languages" (see: Python, Go, Ruby, etc.) are bad choices for writing any code that handles keying material in cryptography. from pathos import multiprocessing, pools as pp
import time


class Multiprocess(object):

    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def qmp_worker(self,(inputs, the_time)):
        print " Processs %s\tWaiting %s seconds" % (inputs, the_time)
        time.sleep(int(the_time))
        print " Process %s\tDONE" % inputs

    def qmp_handler(self):                      # Non tandem pair processing
        pool = pp.ProcessPool(2)
        pool.map(self.qmp_worker, data)


def mp_worker((inputs, the_time)):
    print " Processs %s\tWaiting %s seconds" % (inputs, the_time)
    time.sleep(int(the_time))
    print " Process %s\tDONE" % inputs

def mp_handler():                           # Non tandem pair processing
    p = multiprocessing.Pool(2)
    p.map(mp_worker, data)

def mp_handler_tandem():
    subdata = zip(data[0::2], data[1::2])
    print subdata
    for task1, task2 in subdata:
        p = multiprocessing.Pool(2)
        p.map(mp_worker, (task1, task2))


#data = (['a', '1'], ['b', '2'], ['c', '3'], ['d', '4'])
data = (['a', '2'], ['b', '3'], ['c', '1'], ['d', '4'], ['e', '1'], ['f', '2'], ['g', '3'], ['h', '4'])

if __name__ == '__main__':
    mp_handler()
    mp_handler_tandem()

    Multiprocess().qmp_handler()
```

here
```
if uncomplete:
