You only add write event to_local_sock, but didn't remove read event from _remote_sock,

Here is another example:
1. poll return array like this [(_remote_sock, read), (_local_sock, read)]
2. _remote_sock received 5000 bytes
3. try to send 5000 bytes to _local_sock, but only 2800 bytes success
here event status will changed
_local_sock capture write and error
_remote_sock capture only error
4. _local_sock received 100 bytes (why this will happened? because _local_sock in the array poll returned just after _remote_sock)
5. try to send 100 bytes to _remote_sock, then failed and both connection destroyed

I think you should check data_to_write_to_local and data_to_write_to_remote before destroy, that's the simple way.

OK - so the issue here is `com/twitter/ml/__init__.py` being non-empty and in a strict subpackage of `com/twitter/ml/metrics/__init__.py` and both these packaes being delivered in seperate packaging units.  As such if com/twitter/ml/metrics is loaded 1st, its packages empty `com/twitter/ml/__init__.py` will win and no code in `com/twitter/ml/` from other packages will be visible.  This causes the missing ``com/twitter/ml/ttypes.py` error.  There is not much sane that can be done to compensate on the pants end here.  Right now, for thrift gen, pants does turn empty __init__.py into a namespace package to allow for seperate thrift targets to be combined that share package prefixes.  The best that can be done sanely and generically is to have the PythonChroot look at the global set of local libraries its responsible for unioning and confirming that they are sanely unionable or else failing the chroot construction with a clear message that this is not possible because X targets have differing contents for some package prefix __init__.py. @discordianfish fair question, but I think it basically boils down to a matter of perspective.

from `setup.py`'s perspective, `pex`' and my own, `scripts/snmp_exporter` is not necessarily code at all - but a resource that just gets splatted out to disk and happens to be executable with a `#!` invocation of python.

in `pants`' view of `pex`' today, it executes entrypoints in terms of importable code - not resources. the solution to "pants support[ing] building things without requiring such changes" in this scenario is, as I said, to plumb the "console_scripts as entrypoints" feature of pex to pants which would Just Work™. this is probably a pretty easy PR if you're up for it.

as a general best practice tho, my first suggestion still stands - and works fluidly today. keeping all of your important code importable and making the surface area of your script resources like this as tiny as possible will reap benefits in terms of integration testing and thirdparty integration among other things.

Unfortunately, the SAM translator does not support intrinsic functions in the CodeUri field of `AWS::Serverless::Function` if you are using the S3 URI format of that property. Looking at the code, if it sees the CodeUri is an object (which it always will be if you're using intrinsic functions), it assumes the user is using the form of CodeUri that accepts an [S3 location object](https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/versions/2016-10-31.md#s3-location-object), which is why you're getting that error.

https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/samtranslator/model/sam_resources.py#L336-L343

You should be able to workaround this limitation by using the S3 location format of CodeUri and using your intrinsic functions inside that object. Try this instead:

```
Parameters:
  Version:
     Type: String
     Default: latest
     Description: Lambda version.
MyFunc:
      Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
      Properties:
        FunctionName: MyFuncName
        CodeUri:
          Bucket: my_backet
          Key: !Sub "my_app/${Version}/code.zip"
        Handler: func.handler
        Role: !Sub "arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/RoleName"
        Runtime: python3.6
        Timeout: 300
        MemorySize: 256
```

If you use this form of CodeUri, the SAM translator just validates that you've provided Bucket and Key parameters and will then pass those values onto the [Code](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-lambda-function.html#cfn-lambda-function-code) property of the `AWS::Lambda::Function` resource in the transformed CloudFormation template.

I solved this problem by creating a custom CFN resource that takes in a NoEcho parameter value and returns an encrypted blob to use in the lambda functions environment. The functions have appropriate decrypt/encrypt permissions via the key policy. See this fork: 
https://github.com/jpompe-classy/lambda-backed-cloud-formation-kms-encryption

For the curious, this is how you balance traffic over multiple interface correctly.
Be careful Linux does not balance traffic on IPv6 AFAICS.

/etc/rc.local
---
```
/sbin/ip -4 route delete default
/sbin/ip -4 route add default nexthop via <gateway 1> nexthop via <gateway 2>
```

interfaces
---
```
iface eth0 inet static
       	address <ip 1>
       	netmask <netmask 1>
       	network <network 1>
       	broadcast <broadcast 1>
       	gateway <gateway 1>

       	# extra routes
       	post-up ip route add default via <gateway 1> dev eth0 table 101
       	post-up ip rule add from <network 1>/<netmask 1> lookup 101
       	post-up ip rule add from <network 1>/<netmask 1> to <network 1>/<netmask 1> lookup main

       	pre-down ip rule del from <network 1>/<netmask 1> lookup 101
       	pre-down ip route del default via <gateway 1> dev eth0 table 101
       	pre-down ip rule del from <network 1>/<netmask 1> to <network 1>/<netmask 1> lookup main
```

Thinking through the ELMo serialization piece and saving the weights as part of the model archive -- I can see several advantages to not including the ELMo weights as part of the archive and instead allowing the model archives to depend on multiple files.

1.  The weights file is big.  So if we e.g. save weights at every epoch then it's really inefficient to keep writing them to disk.  Really we should exclude any non-trainable weights from the archive and assume they are available via other files on disk for this reason (e.g. re-saving word embeddings every epoch runs into the same problem).
2.  The ELMo code now also depends on an options file with hyperparameters.  This is so that we can easily change the model to one with different hyperparameters, but the user doesn't need to know the details of how it works.  In any case, the model archive will need this file.
3.  We already have the functionality to download and cache files from S3 automatically.  So if the file paths point to S3 locations for these additional files, the ELMo code will still work properly if a user tries to run a model without the files in the archive.

You've got a few questions in here:

### Can I use arbitrary tagging with the CRF?

Yes.  You just need to pass in a [set of constraints](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/82686c10bdca062a7e41825000c39372de354479/allennlp/modules/conditional_random_field.py#L148-L152) that specify what the valid transitions are in your tag set.  This is only for the `ConditionalRandomField` _module_, though; to use the `CrfTagger` _model_ with a different set of constraints, you'd have to resort to some hackery, redefining [the `crf` field](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/82686c10bdca062a7e41825000c39372de354479/allennlp/models/crf_tagger.py#L83-L92) on the model with a newly-instantiated one with your own constraints.  Instead of doing that, though, it'd probably be easier to just add BMES to our allowed tag set (see below).

### Can I use BMES?

Yes, with a bit of work.  @nelson-liu recently refactored [the code that constructs the constraints above](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/82686c10bdca062a7e41825000c39372de354479/allennlp/modules/conditional_random_field.py#L96-L132) for certain tag sets, so it should be pretty straightforward to add support for BMES there.  PRs welcome.

That handles getting a set of constraints for viterbi decoding in the CRF, but it doesn't handle actually _reading_ BMES tags.  If you've already got a dataset that's tagged with BMES, and a way to read it in, then that should be all you need.  If you're hoping to add BMES to one of our dataset readers, that will probably take changing some code (e.g., [here](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/82686c10bdca062a7e41825000c39372de354479/allennlp/data/dataset_readers/conll2003.py#L129-L135), but it depends on what dataset you want to work with).

Updating with more info:
* Travis uses pandas version 0.13.0 (which is from 2014, and I strongly suggest updating, since Pandas is actively developed and its API changes fast).
* Version 0.13.0 implements the [`rolling_mean()`](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/0.13/generated/pandas.rolling_mean.html?highlight=rolling#pandas.rolling_mean) function from the module level, as in, `pd.rolling_mean(my_df)`, while the `_pandas_rolling_pre_018()` method in utils implements it as `df.rolling_mean()` (**this implementation has never been correct!**)
* Version 0.18.1, released in May 2016, did away with `pd.rolling_mean(df)` and updated it with `df.rolling().mean()`

So here's where I am at with this.  This is what _I_ did.  I am under the impression that others may have done something similar, perhaps with better results, and I would love for what I did to be amended or corrected.  I think it might possible to do this in fewer steps.

Using 1.5.0, I start by installing the platform as usual.  I had bad luck with configuring everything for use without ceph first and _then_ installing, so I opted for a clean, functional installation, which I then mutated into the desired state.

```
deisctl config platform set domain=devcloud.example.com
deisctl install platform
deisctl start platform
```

Then I stop the database, reconfigure any database connection details used by the controller and restart the controller:

```
deisctl stop database

deisctl config database set host=deis-db-nonprod.internal.example.com
deisctl config database set port=5432
deisctl config database set engine=postgresql_psycopg2
deisctl config database set name=deis
deisctl config database set user=deis
deisctl config database set password=foobar

deisctl restart controller
```

Then I want to swap the store gateway for S3.  My understanding is that only the database component (which is now stopped) and the registry use this.  So I reconfigure gateway connection details as follows and restart the registry.  I also use a custom registry image because of #3429 

```
deisctl stop store-gateway@1

deisctl config registry set image=krancour/registry:latest
deisctl config registry set s3region=us-west-1
deisctl config registry set s3accessKey=<my key>
deisctl config registry set s3secretKey=<my secret>
deisctl config registry set s3path=/
deisctl config registry set s3bucket=my-deis-registry-nonprod
deisctl config registry set bucketName=my-deis-registry-nonprod
deisctl config registry set s3encrypt=false
deisctl config registry set s3secure=false

deisctl config store set gateway/accessKey=<my key>
deisctl config store set gateway/secretKey=<my secret>
deisctl config store set gateway/host=s3.amazonaws.com
deisctl config store set gateway/port=80

deisctl restart registry@1
```

Above, note that I had to set some things twice with different keys.  That seems wrong, and it's been a few days since I looked at this, so my memory is fading already, but I think that registry does use every one of those keys at some point.  I could be wrong.

Again, with the goal being to not use ceph, I want to make sure logs are drained to somewhere external:

```
deisctl config logs set drain=syslog://log-nonprod.internal.example.com:514

deisctl restart logger
```

At this point, things seem to function as expected, but with a number of caveats:

* Don't entirely have a handle on why, but attempting to stop the remaining components of the storage subsystem made everything go wonky.  I know that's very vague.  I should have documented exactly what went wrong days ago.  I apologize.

* Some components cannot successfully restart because of dependencies on components I deliberately stopped.

* Something I glossed over is that the db and S3 bucket I connected these components to were seeded with state captured from a backup of another Deis cluster.  I backed up using the usual directions (link below).  There is no mention in the directions of how to backup or restore _builder_ state.  Users of my new cluster learned pretty quickly that although the API, router, etc. all functioned as expected, they were unable to push/build because their keys were absent from the builder.  Using the CLI / API, it was easy enough for each of them to delete and re-add their keys and then things worked, but I mention this only because it's one more (as of yet) undocumented barrier to running Deis as statelessly as possible with storage as a swappable service that can be bound to in the same fashion as a 12factor application.

http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/backing_up_data/

Chances are, someone more in the know than me can boil this down to something more comprehensible.

That makes sense, and the use case(s) I am interested in may not fit this model.

## Use Case 1: Openly discoverable data
For example, I may want to create an endpoint / server that has read access (and discoverability) to the world to serve a public dataset.  I would give it some kind of labels or metadata for others to search and find, and link to it from various data science / version control platforms (kaggle, github, etc.). If the endpoint has to be explicitly shared with me or declared, I don't think I can do this. 

## Use Case 2: Template Endpoints
To extend that use case, let's define something called a template endpoint. A template endpoint serves a particular content type and given the content type, has an expected organization. A creator of an application can define a search to return a set of endpoints that match the template, and have confidence the media / data will conform to the template. Anyone can add a new template and it will be discovered.

Images with Python 3.7.0 (the stable version) have become available.

I've made [a new branch](https://github.com/veox/py-evm/commits/test-core-with-py3.7) and directed it to use this new machine image, but [there are still dependency problems](https://circleci.com/gh/veox/py-evm/134).

Summary:

The `plyvel` Python package [does not have a pre-built `wheel` available for Python 3.7](https://pypi.org/project/plyvel/#files); so the `tox` run attempts to build the package itself; the system packages `libleveldb-dev` and `libgmp-dev` are not installed on the (Debian 9, "Stretch") machine, so the build fails.

After SSH'ing in and installing them, the build still fails, with same error as in https://github.com/wbolster/plyvel/issues/62, i.e. because building `plyvel` requires `leveldb` header version at least 1.20, whereas Stretch has 1.18.

Possible approaches:

1. wait for `plyvel` to get a py3.7 wheel, or help it happen; alternatively,
2. enable the Debian `testing` repo ("Buster"), which [has `libleveldb-dev` 1.20](https://packages.debian.org/search?searchon=names&keywords=libleveldb-dev).

(2) can get very brittle, so IMO best avoid it.

I was going to talk you through this in person to clarify, but let me attempt it here.

The curious part for me here isn't needing to remove packages.  Totally fine with that part.  Once I remove those packages though, I'd expect to remain at python 2.7.11.  Instead removing that set of packages makes me upgrade to python 3.4.0.

Ohhh, ok.  It's probably the enum34 package.  When I remove it, to maintain the rest of the packages I have in my environment, I have to upgrade python to at least 3.4.0 because enum34 is for python < 3.4.

But... just like the openssl downgrade being a problem here, if conda decides it's going to *change* a package for me, I want it to change it to the latest available (and still compatible with my environment).  At least I think I do.

In the specific context of what to do with python here, the "minimum change" strategy puts us at 3.4.0.  That's clearly wrong to me.  It should at least be 3.4.4 (3.4 latest).  However... that's complicated because each package potentially has it's own versioning strategy.  I would be fine here getting python 3.5.1.  (Actually, upgrading to 3.5.1 was going to be my next step after the conda remove anyway.)

So I think the minimum change pass is good, but we might need another pass...  If we *do* need to change the package, then upgrade to the latest that still gives us a consistent environment.

To jump in on the issue of depreciating --name, I think there is a better argument in favor of it: having syntax more closely match semantics.

The current,  syntax `conda create --name envname package name` is odd to someone unfamiliar with it. The primary thing that the command does is create an environment. Its name (or location/prefix) is central to the purpose of the command, but isn't a primary argument. At the same time, conceptually, installing a particular package into that environment is actually not a necessary part of creating an environment, so it should be an option.

The current syntax runs counter to unix conventions because
* --name is an "option" that isn't actually optional.
* `conda create` isn't a single operation but two: create and install.

Compare:
`conda env create fedex --name rainforest` which reads as "invoke conda environment tool to create (with 'fedex') something named rainforest"
 `conda env create rainforest --install fedex` which reads as "invoke conda environment tool to create 'rainforest' and install 'fedex'"

In the general case you wouldn't want to `conda install` or `conda create` with packages from a different platform. There are some corner cases, e.g., `post-link.sh` scripts that can run arbitrary code as a post-installation step. Those scripts could, for example, test on which platform they are running and then run macOS-specific code in your Linux-targeted environment. So what you actually want is to collect some data (e.g., package lists or even the package tarballs) but not install those -- that's also what `constructor` does when it builds the installers.
You can try the following scripts (make sure to use the `--download-only` and `--dry-run` options!):
```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -ex
DEST_SUBDIR=osx-64
DEST_PACKAGES=libcxx
WORK_DIR=work
(
    mkdir -p $WORK_DIR
    cd $WORK_DIR
    # pretend to be on another platform
    export CONDA_SUBDIR=$DEST_SUBDIR
    # specify where to download packages
    export CONDA_PKGS_DIRS=./$DEST_SUBDIR
    # download packages to WORK_DIR
    conda create --download-only --yes --name unused-env-name $DEST_PACKAGES
    # use conda-build to turn WORK_DIR into a Conda channel
    conda index .
    # test if all requested packages are installed from WORK_DIR
    conda create --dry-run --name unused-env-name --channel ./ $DEST_PACKAGES
)
ls -la ./$WORK_DIR
```
I wouldn't assume this is a supported use case and YMMV. This, however, only gives you the packages in a local channel; you'll still need a `conda` installation on the target platform. If you really want a full-fledged installer, you should use `constructor` (if you experience issues with `constructor`, please file an issue at its issue tracker).

Hmmm.  This is an awkward edge case.  The Voluptuous schema validator cannot coerce a string value of "True" to being a boolean value of `True`.  This is because Voluptuous' coerce method doesn't care about string boolean values:

```
def coerce(type, msg=None):
"""Coerce a value to a type.
 
If the type constructor throws a ValueError, the value will be marked as
Invalid.
"""
    def f(v):
        try:
            return type(v)
        except ValueError:
            raise Invalid(msg or ('expected %s' % type.__name__))
    return f
```

You can see what happens at any python command line:

```
>>> l = ['True', 'true', True, 'False', 'false', False]
>>> print('{0}'.format(l))
['True', 'true', True, 'False', 'false', False]
>>> for v in l:
...     print('Value: {0} is of type: {1}'.format(v, type(v)))
...
Value: True is of type: <type 'str'>
Value: true is of type: <type 'str'>
Value: True is of type: <type 'bool'>
Value: False is of type: <type 'str'>
Value: false is of type: <type 'str'>
Value: False is of type: <type 'bool'>
```

So because environment variables are inherently strings, Voluptuous cannot coerce.  What this means is that in order for it to coerce a string value of `'true'|'True'` or `'false'|'False'` into a true bool, I need to do it myself.  I can do this, and I apologize for the inconvenience.

The issue is that Curator requires the elasticsearch python client and it is trying to find it on pypi. You can follow the guidelines listed in the following link to ensure all dependencies are installed.

> I read today that ES doesn't reccomand using same repo for long time since the recovery will take longer than without snapshots.

Please let me know where you read this.  I would like to see this recommendation in print, as it is not something we commonly recommend.  

Recovery only takes a long time when there are hundreds of thousands of segments.  This can easily be avoided by using a 2, or perhaps 3 tiered approach.  Frequent repository rollover should _not_ be necessary if this is handled correctly.  In fact, it can result in a lot of extra data storage cost, as data may be duplicated in multiple locations.

When using time-series data, we _do_ recommend having a "fast moving" snapshot repository.  This would have hourly or daily snapshots, but only persist them for a few days.  After your time-series indices rollover, they're unlikely to be changed, so you can do a "forceMerge," which is an optimize call in Curator 3.  It is recommended to forceMerge to 1 or 2 segments per shard, where feasible.  For snapshotting force merged segments, a separate repository would be recommended, because it only contains segments unlikely to change, so incremental segment creep wouldn't happen.

If you specify name, then the assumption is that the gateway exists in the same resource group as your VMSS (but it will still use existing if it does in fact exist there).

Some other tricky bits about creating a VMSS with an existing app gateway (so you can try with an AG in the same RG or after this bug is fixed):

Your app gateway has a subnet that its frontend IP config is associated with. You will need to create a second subnet in that same VNet.  This will be the VNet and subnet that you supply when you run your `vmss create` command. So your flow would look something like:

```
az network application-gateway create ...
az network vnet subnet create --vnet-name {ag vnet name} -n VMSSsubnet --address-prefix {subnet within ag vnet}
az vmss create --app-gateway {ag} --vnet-name {ag vnet name} --subnet VMSSsubnet

```

The implementation of `get_resource_name` looks correct to me:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/69da9801a7abb98b322df60ce4fcc0410863eccf/azure-common/testutils/common_recordingtestcase.py#L98

The name becomes redundant if you pass in the qualified test name as the `name` parameter, which is what is done here:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/94ed6ed3858e00420b95e244860c87e692d4a6b4/azure-mgmt/tests/mgmt_testcase.py#L70

It probably doesn't need to pass in the fully qualified name there, it could pass in a shorter "prefix" instead.

Note that there is some value (at least there was back then) to having a name that can be traced back to a particular test/test class. When you use the Azure portal and see a ton of resources or resource groups that didn't clean up properly, it's nice to be able to tell where they came from.  If the full name is just a checksum, it's more difficult.

@migurski I've made a minor change to make regex much easier to handle. Instead of depending on look-around functions it uses capture groups. The captured groups are returned while the non-capture groups are ignored.

This means that a replacement for current fixtures could be as simple as:
```
number: ([0-9]+)
street: (?:[0-9]+ )(.*)
```

In the street case since the first group is a non-capture group it is used to make the match but not included in the final result. Our problem is purely one of logistics. 

We define our world by reading input files (urdf/sdf).  As we read specifications for collision geometry we push it into the collision world (very much an on-the-fly) kind of thing.  After we're done reading the file, we do some post processing to evaluate what we've read in and classify which objects should actually be filtered out.  By the end of this post-processing, the object filter now has enough smarts to recognize when a pair should be filtered out.  But the damage has already been done. 

So, in our case, it's not that we're particularly changing the collision objects.  The problem is that the code was written without an understanding of Bullet's internal workings.  The thinking was that as long as the filter was in place *before* collision queries were performed it was good.  This was a mistake.

So, what we have to do is accumulate all of the collision geometry, analyze it, and then, finally, add it to the collision world now that the overlap filter has sufficient data to provide the desired effect.

Make sense?

This is a kind of review.
At first, thank you so much @N-Coder 
Very impressive work on timeout problem discovering.
While I not agree with all design decision without this PR we even had no point to start discussion about the feature implementation.

I very like `RequestTimeouts` public API.
But I complain the `metadata`.
Please let me describe my position.
I used to apply meta-programming everywhere, it reduces code lines amount, sure.
But later I've figured out that code lines saving is nothing from both *code reading* and **debugging** perspective.
Mental capacity is limited, explicit call very often is more obvious than `getattr` by attribute name and making a call. Debugging is complicated too obviously.
Let's keep `aiohttp` code *easy to read* even if we need to write more code.

Also this is the reason why I want to separate client timeouts from client tracing signals.
They are overlaps, sure (but not entirely: `read_timeout` is a timeout between receiving a data from connecting peer, there is no corresponding signal *brackets* like `on_connection_create_start` / `on_connection_create_end`.

Moreover I strongly suggest to not use `gather` for client tracing: the `Signal` is explicitly and intentionally a list of async callbacks. The list is controlled by user, if a list callback has failed -- the whole HTTP request must fail.
Tracing libraries (aiohttp tracing facility clients) are responsible to not raise an exception if they are not supposed to do.
Let's suppose we have a *tracer* which modifies HTTP headers. Why? I have no idea. Like I don't assume that the server middleware doesn't modify request/response.
It means we should apply tracing callbacks in user controlled order.

At the end thank you again for raising such important questions not for only timeouts implementation but for aiohttp design principles at all.

The exchange directory has carefully crafted permissions to prevent students from accessing and modifying each others' assignments. It's not well document, but hopefully we won't need such a complicated solution soon anyway 😄 

Here's the detailed explanation of how it works:

The toplevel exchange directory has 777 permissions, but the subdirectories have different permissions. The inbound directory is writable by all, but not readable or listable by anybody but the instructor. This has the effect that students can write to the directory in order to submit their assignments, but cannot see anybody else's submissions. The outbound directory is readable by all but not writable by anybody but the instructor, so that only the instructor can release assignments:

```
$ ls -l /srv/nbgrader/exchange/cogsci131/
total 272
drwx-ws-wx 3968 instructor instructor 270336 Jan 17 04:22 inbound
drwxr-xr-x   21 instructor instructor   4096 Dec  5 16:56 outbound
```

Submission *directories* in the inbound directory are readable and writeable by everybody (though note because the inbound directory is not readable by anyone except the instructor, no one but the instructor can actually read or list these directories):

```
$ ls -l /srv/nbgrader/exchange/cogsci131/inbound/
total 15864
drwxrwxrwx 4 student1 instructor 4096 Dec 12 07:46 student1+final+2016-12-12 07:46:33 UTC
```

But the individual files within the submission directories are writable only by the student, which means that even if another student tried to remove the submission directory, they would not have permission to remove the files inside it:

```
$ ls -l /srv/nbgrader/exchange/cogsci131/inbound/student1+final+2016-12-12\ 07\:46\:33\ UTC/
total 524
-rw-r--r-- 1 student1 instructor   3946 Dec  5 16:56 0 - Index.ipynb
...
```

Released assignments are only writable by the instructor, but readable by everybody:

```
$ ls -l /srv/nbgrader/exchange/cogsci131/outbound/
total 76
drwxr-xr-x 4 instructor instructor 4096 Dec  5 10:23 final
$ ls -l /srv/nbgrader/exchange/cogsci131/outbound/final/
total 144
-rw-r--r-- 1 instructor instructor  3946 Dec  5 16:56 0 - Index.ipynb
```

Is it possible that something blocks PUT requests? They're less common than GET and POST, but still not exactly unusual.

The GET request you see is to check the last-modified time of the file on disk against the last save, in case the file has been changed by something else. The PUT request does the actual save, and the POST request creates a checkpoint after a manual save (you shouldn't see this one after an autosave).

I have run into this issue also a couple of times. Some of this can be stated as the general problem of transferring data to the browser.

For individual kernels:
- images can be transferred as data  URIs
- comm messages can be used, with or without messages. The drawback is that binary data has to be escaped and coded to fit into the "json over websocket" paradigm
- The kernel can output html/javascript code which then can be executed or picked up by the front-side code. Same problem about encoding, and additionally the kernel can't really control where in the notebook the output is going.
- Use Tornado or Asyncio to run your own server, optionally in another thread. No encoding problems, can implement any REST API it wants, even additional websockets. The problem here is the additional port that needs to be opened and managed. It's not trivial for the kernel to figure out under which IP address this port is accessible.

In my opinion, using comm messages is the best method, because it requires no additional configuration. Sometimes though, this isn't possible, or very difficult, because certain Javascript APIs aren't flexible enough to take data from other front-side code, or it would be too complicated to convert them from a REST API.

A notebook extension could act as a reverse proxy to the kernel's HTTP server. The notebook server would provide a URL base and forward the requests. This would solve problems about ports and addresses. It would be tricky to support advanced HTTP features like websockets or audio/video streaming, but these would also be possible.

I think you're reusing the master branch for a series of pull requests, merging our master branch into it each time.

We can resolve that here, but it's neater to make a new branch for each change you want to submit. Something like this:

```shell
git clone https://github.com/jupyter/notebook.git
cd notebook
git remote add PHaeJin https://github.com/PHaeJin/notebook.git

# Make a new branch
git checkout -b typo1

# Make your changes, then commit
git commit -m "Fix a typo"

git push -u PHaeJin typo1
```

That way you won't have an ever-increasing number of commits in each pull request.

There are two (possibly three) problems with the `--prefix` argument.

First, it overrides the default installation directory structure.  The
`--root` argument doesn't, it keeps the exact same directory structure.
This allows you to just pick up the entire root from your build directory
and put in the the real root later when doing a package installation and
everything will be like the `--root` argument wasn't used at all.

Second, the `--prefix` argument doesn't affect the settings directory (as
far as I can tell looking at the source).  The `--root` argument controls
every directory, they are all placed relative to `--root`.  Again, this
lets you transparently move everything at once to its final location.

Another difference, which may or may not be an issue with any nbextensions
but often is with other installations, is that the change of root is
invisible to whatever is installed.  So if what is installed keeps track of
where it is installed, it won't see the `--root` directory, it will think
it is still being installed relative to the normal root.  This makes sure
that the applications are portable when the root is changed during package
installation later.

Just to clarify things a bit: we are talking about three different cases here:

1. the original image is correctly classified and the attack finds an adversarial:

- the adversarial is returned
- no warning produced
- no exception thrown

2. the original image already fulfills the _criterion_ for an adversarial (e.g. is misclassified or classified as some target class, etc.) and therefore the attack is not executed

- the original image is returned as the adversarial
- a UserWarning is produced to make the user aware of this special case
- no exception is thrown

3. the original image is correctly classified but the attack does not find an adversarial

- None is returned
- a UserWarning is produced to make it more obvious to the user, especially during interactive usage
- no exception is thrown


Now I think everyone is fine with 1.
For 2. and 3. one can discuss whether it's more appropriate to throw an error instead of returning the original image (2. case) or None (3. case). I think we have four options:

a) leave everything as is
b) only change the 2. case to throw an error instead of returning something
c) only change the 3. case to throw an error instead of returning something
d) change the 2. and 3. case to throw errors instead of returning something

For the 2. case, it's a question of how one defines **adversarial**, i.e. whether one is okay with treating original images as adversarials if they fulfill the _criterion_, despite not being close to a correctly classified image. If yes then returning the original image is correct behavior, if no (more traditional view on adversarials) then an error should be thrown.

For the 3. case, it's a question of whether one thinks of failing attacks as something exceptional that should usually not happen (should thrown an error) or as something normal (should return None).


In Python, both semantics (returning a special value like None or the original image vs. throwing an error) are common and accepted; afaik there is no preferred _pythonic_ way. My impression is that people usually prefer special return values (e.g. None) because they seem easier to handle, but at the same time, exceptions are more explicit and enforce a response from the user.


At the moment, I think I have a slight preference for _throwing errors_, but I am not decided. It would make non-interactive usage more verbose:

```python3
try:
    adversarial = attack(image, label)
except OriginalImageIsAdversarial:
    adversarial = image  # for example; user has the ability to handle this differently
except NoAdversarialFound:
    # handle failed attack ...
```

instead of

```python3
adversarial = attack(image, label)
if adversarial is None:
    # handle failed attack ...
```

@j-towns , you're raising excellent points. I'm living in the past. There was a brief period of Autograd's history when we separated the [value-storing `Node`](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/0f026abd25a7c8fb182a/autograd/core.py#L219) from the [graph-representing `ReverseNode`](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/0f026abd25a7c8fb182a/autograd/core.py#L208). In fact, this sort of memory optimization was the whole motivation for the `ReverseNode`. In those days, the vjp functions returned a closure and Python was clever enough not to store those variables that weren't used in the inner function. The only data structure you'd end up after evaluating your composite function was a tape of ReverseNodes, with the values themselves stored implicitly in the closures as needed. The sad thing is that we ended up adding `value` to `ReverseNode` to track (generalized) shape information, which completely defeated the purpose. Now that we have vspaces, we wouldn't need to do that any more.

So it looks like what I'm proposing would require re-introducing some sort of `ReverseNode`. The memory saving are pretty compelling so it might be worth it. And we have benchmarks now to see whether the overhead is really anything to worry about. We could even go back to the nested functions of `defgrad` but I feel bad constantly swapping out the the interface from under people's feet.

@tkdchen you should really describe what you need to do in the appication. GSSAPI is intentionally a more abstract API, but can generally do all that is needed.
If you use a modern MIT Kerberos distribution for example you can use the credentials store extensions to operate exactly on the ketabs and ccaches you want through the gss_aqcuire_cred_from/gss_store_creds_into() extension calls. These calls are available via python-gssapi if the underlying GSSAPI library supports them.

You can force the intialization of a ccache for example by Acquiring credentials specifying a name and a keytab, and then inspecting the returned Credentials object.
I am goign by memory but it would look something like:

Using default, name (as retrieved from the default keytab), default keytab and default ccache:
```
creds = gssapi.creds.Credentials(usage='initiate')
if creds.lifetime > 0:
    return true
```

Using default keytab and ccache, but choosing a specific principal name:
```
principal = gssapi.names.Name('HTTP/hostname@REALM',
                                                   gssapi.raw.NameType.kerberos_principal)
creds = gssapi.creds.Credentials(name=principal, usage='initiate')
```

Specifying a custom name, keytab and ccache:
```
store = {'ccache': 'FILE:/some/ccache/file', 'keytab':'/etc/some.keytab' }
principal = gssapi.names.Name('HTTP/hostname@REALM',
                                                   gssapi.raw.NameType.kerberos_principal)
creds = gssapi.creds.Credentials(name=principal, usage='initiate', store=store)
```

The purpose of DANE and HPKP is to reduce the chance of MITM attacks. Reducing the attack surface from the large number of CAs that you know little about to one CA that you've dealt with is a significant improvement. It allows you to avoid MITM attacks from CAs you don't trust. HPKP and DANE wouldn't support pinning intermediate certificates unless it provided a significant trust benefit.

Let's assume however that we don't want to trust any CAs, even Let's Encrypt.

You just stated that HPKP and DNSSec is *very* fragile and requires manual review. If it takes that much effort, it's unclear how this can compare to the one-time effort of adding a single command line option to the script that calls letsencrypt to use a CSR you generated. If things need to be easier, the answer is to add an option that forces the client to reuse the same CSR, not to issue certificates with longer lifetimes.

Yes, DANE supports pinning by a specific certificate. When does this provide a security advantage over pinning by public key? Since you've removed the CA from the trust model, a certificate is compromised only if its private key is compromised, which means you'd have to regenerate based on a new private key in both cases. Pinning by public key rather than by certificate is useful for when you have a site that has a extremely strict certificate rotation policy but are reusing the same keypair. Since you don't, there's no benefit from certificate pinning over public key pinning.

I acknowledge that scripting based on cert-pinning would be difficult, but it appears to have no benefit over public-key pinning as stated above.

@dhirschfeld Rather than instantiating a `parser` object, if you use the `parse()` function it will work. Alternatively, you can `encode` the string so that it is unicode before passing it to `dateutil.parser.parser.parse()`, or use `from __future__ import unicode_literals`:

    >>>from dateutil.parser import parse, parser
    >>>p = parser()
    >>>parse('01-Jan-2015')
    datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1, 0, 0)
    >>>p.parse(u'01-Jan-2015')
    datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1, 0, 0)

The reason for this is that the tokenizer requires unicode strings - `parse()` has logic that handles the conversion, but this is not included in `parser.parse()` (for which `parse()` is a light wrapper). It seems reasonable to me that we could move the unicode-to-string logic into `parser.parse()` or even into `_timelex()`, but I'm not sure if that was an oversight or if there's a specific reason it was done this way. Maybe @jarondl knows.

For now, if you really need to call `parse()` on a parser object, you can validate the input string as such:

```python
    from six import binary_type       
    from dateutil.parser import parser

    p = parser()
    timestr = '01-Jan-2015'
    if isinstance(timestr, binary_type):
        timestr = timestr.decode()
    p.parse(timestr)
```

For most use cases, though, it is sufficient to pass a `parserobject` as a second (or named) argument to `dateutil.parser.parse`.

@kkroo This is partially a duplicate of #58, though your actual problem is that you have included a 3-digit millisecond value in your timestamp, which is not a format recognized by the parser. The parser counts the digits, sees that it's not in a recognizable format and decides that it must be the year, then passes that number to `datetime`.

```python
from dateutil import parser
parser.parse('20160407061740')
datetime.datetime(2016, 4, 7, 6, 17, 40)
```

I think a format more like `'20160407T061740.404'` would be advisable.

One thing to consider is that, as implemented, `within_delta` uses "wall clock" arithmetic, so two instants 1 minute apart in absolute time but 61 minutes apart in "wall time" will *not* be within a 30 minute delta. It might be a good idea to check if `dt1` and `dt2` are tz-aware and if so convert them to UTC first.

> This is due to common-workflow-language/v1.0/v1.0/underscore.js which isn't mentioned anywhere in the reporting; can that be fixed?
Should this have passed validation?

These errors are generated because: jshint is not taking in to account `if(typeof ... === "undefined")` type statements (issue for this is jshint: https://github.com/jshint/jshint/issues/1986), underscore is using `setTimeout` and `clearTimeout`, which are not in the ES5.1 spec and jshint is not fully understanding the statement `root._ = _` [see here](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/common-workflow-language/blob/83650a5b6b34c0648a20742eb02b475865555f50/v1.0/v1.0/underscore.js#L51) as creating a new variable `_`. This could be fixed to not output errors by: a) using `--disable-js-validation` b) disabling `W117` (undefined name checking), by using `--js-hint-options-file` for anything which uses `underscore.js`. I enabled the `W117`, as it is very useful for detecting errors in simple JavaScript expressions, but for a piece of code which is a large and dynamic as underscore.js, warnings are probably going to come up

If unplugging and plugging-in the same device is involved, it may also be an electronics issue.

Electronic devices need a reset to properly start up. They also need energy and use capacitors to store it. If  a device is unplugged, capacitors discharge, the speed of that may depend on the state the device was in (open/closed). If the power comes back and the capacitors are not fully discharged, it may happen that a device does not reset properly.

So if you unplug that device and wait much longer before plugging in again, does the problem still show up?

This is turning into a fairly difficult problem to solve; tastypie was relying heavily on `QUERY_TERMS` to quickly pre-validate filters.  There doesn't appear to be a graceful way to regenerate that set of operators without overhauling `ModelResource.build_filters`.  This is long overdue, anyway; validating against a constant list of operators made it impossible to add more operators without monkey-patching, and the global list of operators meant it would allow nonsensical operations through for fields that make no sense, like a case-insensitive filter on an integer field.

The path forward is probably rebuilding `build_filters` to retrieve the Django field and use the new lookups api to validate the operation.

Docs are lacking and the API is a bit weird, but let me try to clear things a
up a bit:

ID3 isn't tied to any media format and can be attached to any file. In case
there is not ID3 tag there is nothing to load which is why loading fails.

If you want to handle files without tags you can create an empty tag instance and
add it to the file:

```python
from mutagen.id3 import ID3, ID3NoHeaderError

try:
    tags = ID3("song.mp3")
except ID3NoHeaderError:
    tags = ID3()

# to something with tags

tags.save("song.mp3")
```

or you can use the MP3 class:

```python
from mutagen.mp3 import MP3

mp3 = MP3("song.mp3")
if mp3.tags is None:
    mp3.add_tags()
tags = mp3.tags

# to something with tags

mp3.save()
```

Note that loading and saving can still raise MutagenError due to
corrupt/invalid files or I/O errors.

Hi Martin,

Yes, the SMTLIB-proposed encoding is a bit cumbersome, but ultimately not that hard to do. During all other encoding is going on, I usually keep a vector of expressions for "side conditions" where all such additional things go, and in the end when everything is done, I add all those additional things to the solver/goal/etc. You do have to introduce variables (like b), but they are not much more expensive than asking the solver to introduce variables and then hide their names (e.g., after FPA to BV conversion, the result is virtually identical). 

Using quantifiers to axiomatize these functions is definitely a bad idea, way too expensive and fragile (Z3 can solve your formula, but will not do it with the default options (mbqi instantiation limit too small) and it's not fast).

I really wouldn't want to add another special instruction or to modify the existing semantics of to_ieee_bv. In fact, I'd much rather remove to_ieee_bv and then add whatever the SMTLIB people decide to use; FPA is complicated enough. Also, do not assume that SMT solvers use the encoding into bit-vectors that is proposed in the IEEE standard - one of the objectives of the SMT standard was not to prescribe any encoding, such that solvers are free to use whatever thechniques they think make sense. Z3 happens to use a similar encoding (but not identical), so for me adding to_ieee_bv was easy, but for others it may be much harder to add, than it is to ask you to use additional variables and assertions :)

Also, note that in SMT-FP there is always _exactly_ one NaN, and it has _no_ predefined bit-vector equivalent (since there is no SMT-compliant function to translate it). Also, the IEEE standard only proposes encodings for particular float types (half, single, double, quadruple, etc), but SMT FP allows any number of significand/exponent bits, and I'm sure Z3 still contains bugs that are only triggered by weird combinations for which the "generalized" IEEE encoding is a disadvantage. 

The implementation of to_ieee_bv is almost surely buggy somewhere as it has undergone very little testing. The fact that it returns 0 for NaN is full expected though. Z3 supports two ways of treating undefined results for all fp.to_* functions: Either keep an uninterpreted function, or just return 0 (similar to the "hardware interpretation" of bit-vector operations). At this point, just returning 0 is the default operation, but I may change that now that I have at least 1 example of a person who uses it. The magic option is rewriter.hi_fp_unspecified which is true by default. Of course, if you disable this option, Z3 will not be able to use a pure SAT solver anymore and it will have to fall back to a solver that supports uninterpreted functions (we do have a roughly "practical" solution for unspecified bit-vectors by eager ackermannization, but that's not available for FPA yet).
Update: There is now also [BTRW15](http://smtlib.cs.uiowa.edu/papers/BTRW15.pdf). Not sure that's the latest version though.

Exporting this to JSON shouldn't be a problem. For using it in ConvNetJS, you'll need to define a network of the same structure in ConvNetJS, then assign the weights in correct order. The list of params will go from the lowest layers (near the input) to the highest layers (near the output). For every layer, it first lists the weights, then the bias (if any). Carefully check if the weights for ConvNetJS follow the same layout as for Lasagne; if not, you may need to swap some of the axes.

Hint: If you name all your layers (by passing `name='xyz'` to the layer constructor), then all the weights will have clear names, so you can get them with `[p.name for p in lasagne.layers.get_all_params(network)]`. This will help you to get the order correct, or to export them as a dictionary instead of a list.

If you figure out a way that works, we'd welcome a toy example in [Lasagne/Recipes](https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes)!

Hm, it depends on the context and your personal taste. From some point of view, the object is serialized - you have field and its value (``Money`` instance). Further, if you want to convert the serialized result to JSON you could create your own ``JSONEncoder`` (like Django does) and convert this value to some valid JSON output.

Another option - return dictionary from this method instead of Money, but it will be nested structure. In case if you want "flat" structure DRF implies, that you should use separate field for this. But you can unnest this structure later in the serialization process, but it is a bit hacky IMO. 

So, there are a plenty of options to do serialization :)

This usually produces the exception, sometimes in as few as 500 seconds, sometimes in as much as 6000 seconds. The magic numbers (ttl=300, 4 threads) seem to be relatively important because trying to change them makes it harder to reproduce (I had no luck with ttl=15 nor with ttl=3000). The exception leaves messages like this in the Postgres log:

```
ERROR:  deadlock detected
DETAIL:  Process 20392 waits for ShareLock on transaction 13130988; blocked by process 20391.
        Process 20391 waits for ShareLock on transaction 13130989; blocked by process 20392.
        Process 20392: DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl;
        Process 20391: DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl;
HINT:  See server log for query details.
CONTEXT:  while deleting tuple (482,68) in relation "cache"
STATEMENT:  DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl;
```

Finding this unhelpful, I added information to the query to make it clearer which "get" query prompted the DELETE:

```
ERROR:  deadlock detected
DETAIL:  Process 5119 waits for ShareLock on transaction 13272262; blocked by process 5117.
        Process 5117 waits for ShareLock on transaction 13272270; blocked by process 5119.
        Process 5119: DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl; -- 'fxa.oauth.verify_token:993e1f0b77d15d1ac958d4f687959eec6620cbce2684c718eaad2a4efa36513a:[''sync:addon_storage'']'
        Process 5117: DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl; -- 'Ethan is testing4822691'
HINT:  See server log for query details.
CONTEXT:  while deleting tuple (167,97) in relation "cache"
STATEMENT:  DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl; -- 'fxa.oauth.verify_token:993e1f0b77d15d1ac958d4f687959eec6620cbce2684c718eaad2a4efa36513a:[''sync:addon_storage'']'
```

As the logs indicate, both transactions are blocked on a ShareLock. Mathieu didn't find the ShareLocks for two reasons -- one, the lock doesn't show up in `pg_locks` until the second transaction is blocked on it, and two, the query he used excludes locks from `pg_locks` like this one, with `relation` being NULL. However, here's what it looks like:

```
testdb=# select * from pg_locks;
   locktype    | database | relation | page | tuple | virtualxid | transactionid | classid | objid | objsubid | virtualtransaction |  pid  |
        mode         | granted | fastpath 
---------------+----------+----------+------+-------+------------+---------------+---------+-------+----------+--------------------+-------+
---------------------+---------+----------
 relation      |    16401 |    61705 |      |       |            |               |         |       |          | 14/685             | 16268 |
 RowExclusiveLock    | t       | t

 RowExclusiveLock    | t       | t
 relation      |    16401 |    61697 |      |       |            |               |         |       |          | 14/685             | 16268 |

 ExclusiveLock       | t       | t

 ShareLock           | f       | f       # <-- this one
 transactionid |          |          |      |       |            |      13272541 |         |       |          | 14/685             | 16268 |

From my reading (e.g. [this one](http://www.moioli.net/progetti/postgres-deadlocks-debugging-guidelines/)), the first transaction doesn't generate a lock because it can tell that the row hasn't been modified by any current transaction; instead it just optimistically updates it. The second transaction wants to grab a row lock on some rows, and this turns into a transaction lock.

It seems like a ShareLock is the kind of lock that is acquired when trying to determine whether a row matches a `WHERE` clause in some other query. So the second `DELETE` query is determining whether it's going to delete the rows deleted by the first query (which could still rollback).

A deadlock only occurs when two transactions grab locks on objects in a different order. In this case, it seems clear that the objects being locked are rows, and it seems like the queries that are grabbing locks are the `DELETE` queries. So why are the locks being grabbed in a different order? I don't 100% understand the query plans given, but it seems that the `DELETE` query is visiting rows in physical order rather than according to the index, because:

```
testdb=# explain (verbose) delete from cache where ttl is not null and '2017-08-08 19:54:06' > ttl;
                                                        QUERY PLAN                                                        
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Delete on public.cache  (cost=234.32..2595.19 rows=4869 width=6)
   ->  Bitmap Heap Scan on public.cache  (cost=234.32..2595.19 rows=4869 width=6)
         Output: ctid
         Recheck Cond: ((cache.ttl IS NOT NULL) AND ('2017-08-08 19:54:06'::timestamp without time zone > cache.ttl))
         ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_cache_ttl  (cost=0.00..233.11 rows=4869 width=0)
               Index Cond: ((cache.ttl IS NOT NULL) AND ('2017-08-08 19:54:06'::timestamp without time zone > cache.ttl))
(6 rows)
```

(A bitmap index scan is explained a bit [here](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/static/indexes-bitmap-scans.html).) Note that it generates different query plans for 'timestamp' < ttl vs. 'timestamp' > ttl, and it also generates different query plans for timestamps that are less than/greater than the entire table.

In order to explore this a little better, I've been running DELETE queries using data generated with this function:

```plpgsql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.insert_junk()
 RETURNS void
 LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $function$
BEGIN
FOR i in 1 .. 100000 LOOP
  INSERT INTO cache VALUES ('Ethan is testing ' || random() * 100000, i, sec2ttl(random() * 300 + 45)) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
END LOOP;
END;
$function$
```

The `value` field of the database shows its insert order, which appears to match physical order (doing a `SELECT * FROM cache LIMIT 10` shows the ones with value 1..10).

If both `DELETE` queries are locking rows in physical order, then it shouldn't be possible to induce a deadlock with just two `DELETE` queries. Therefore my suspicion, although I haven't reproduced it yet, is that one `UPDATE` is happening, then a `DELETE` is blocking on that `UPDATE`, and then a series of other `UPDATE`s are happening which change the physical order of the rows, such that when a second `DELETE` happens, it manages to grab some rows which weren't grabbed by the first `DELETE`. Then, when the first `UPDATE` commits, you get a deadlock.

I'm still trying to reproduce the thing with a clear set of queries, aided by increasingly verbose logging on my local postgres and the occasional reproduced deadlock. However, the real question is what to do about it. [Here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/27007196/avoiding-postgresql-deadlocks-when-performing-bulk-update-and-delete-operations/27015834#27015834) is a solution that uses `SELECT FOR UPDATE` to lock rows according to an `ORDER BY` (i.e. a deterministic order that doesn't depend on physical order or change between transactions). That's probably the most targeted solution.

This is also an opportunity to rethink this part of the code more generally. @leplatrem and I both don't like that this `get()` operation, which has a very read-only sort of name, does a giant delete. To preserve correctness, we could add a `AND now() < ttl` to the `SELECT` statement to ensure we only get cache entries that haven't expired yet, and move the `DELETE` someplace else -- a "cron job" sort of thing that happens only once every N seconds, or once every N requests, or with probability xxx%, or something else entirely. (A database trigger?) Alternately, we could replace the giant `DELETE` with smaller deletes, perhaps having the `get()` method only delete outdated entries for its own key. If we can be assured that only one `DELETE` is running at a time, or that each `DELETE` only locks one row, then we don't really need to care too much about the order in which the `DELETE` locks the rows, and we wouldn't have to do the `SELECT FOR UPDATE` thing. On the other hand, it seems that we can get the error with only a few simultaneous requests, so maybe we should do the `SELECT FOR UPDATE` thing too.

I'd say:

1. For now I'd say keeping ``transform`` and ``featurize_many()`` is OK.
2. If I understand correctly, this means every single featurizer (i.e. dozens so far) will need to (i) add n_jobs as a parameter in the constructor and (ii) need to call the superclass.__init__(n_jobs=n_jobs) in the constructor. This seems just onerous to me given that most people writing featurizers don't want to worry about n_jobs at all. I'd prefer something else than going through every featurizer and adding n_jobs and making sure the constructor calls the superclass (in my experience, this is the type of developer friction that leads to frustration and mistakes). We've done the work of making n_jobs painless from the developer perspective since once you implement ``featurize()``, ``featurize_many()`` basically needs to be parallelizable. This might be bending the rules of sklearn's BaseEstimator, but we could for example add a ``set_njobs`` method to BaseFeaturizer itself that sets an internal variable. Then all downstream featurizers will automatically inherit it and the ability to set n_jobs without the constructor param. If a featurizer wants to enforce n_jobs=1, it can call set_njobs=1 in the constructor and then override the set_njobs() function with an error message.
3. Yes, I think the implementation guide is fine there!

As @qingzhouzhen pointed out, you need to use python3 (specifically, I have tested it with python3.5 and 3.6). For general package sanity, we recommend using a virtualenv (this will also prevent conflicts between python2 and 3 if you are mainly using former on your machine).

To create and activate the virtualenv, run the following commands:

``` 
    pip install virutalenv
    virtualenv env --python=python3 
    . env/bin/activate
    
```
Whether python3 is being used in a current env can be tested by running `python --version`
After that, `pip install -e .` should succeed, and the installation can be tested using pytest runner as follows:

```
    pip install pytest
    pytest
```

On ubuntu:

the dmag magic functionality looks very straightforward in the notebook.  For it to be totally smooth with varying contributions, we'll want to add in a few kinds of checks.  1., that the expected tables are present, and 2. that the expected column names are present.  I've often been burned by issues like that as contributions are so varied and sometimes are missing even required columns.  

How about if we use the template of dmag_magic to create a more general function that does the importing, merging, and fishing out of the relevant data for plotting by site? Hey, yes this is a good idea. We can do one of the following,

- Modify `validate()` for `i{8,16,32,64}` `TypeSpec`s to raise `ValueError` if the value is out of bounds, and `validate()` for `StructSpec` to wrap `{Value,Type}Error`s to include the field name.
- Modify `StructSpec.to_wire()` to wrap `OverflowError` and include the field name in the message.

I'm partial to the first solution because it doesn't specialize behavior to `OverflowError` and also doesn't add a catch-all-exceptions behavior. It seems generally better to make this part of the `validate()` step. Also, since raising `{Value,Type}Error` is part of the contract of `validate()` (see [docs](https://thriftrw.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#thriftrw.spec.TypeSpec.validate)) so we're not going too wide or too specific with the exceptions.

The code actually invoked is the latest version of `test_cast.py`/`test_cast.cpp`, posted in #376, but with the C++/Python/mk_untracked cases commented out.  Basically what each does is:

- `py_ref` is code that does a tight loop in python, each loop calling a bound function that updates and returns the same instance of a simple pybind-registered type using `return_value_policy::reference`.
- `C++ w/ lambda` passes a Python lambda to a C++ function (as a `pybind11::object`), then does the looping and object creation in C++, with a call to the `py::object`'s call operator inside the loop.
- `mk_copy` is similar to `py_ref`, but it makes a copy of the returned instance via `return_value_policy::copy`.

Some food for thought.

Here's an implementation of 2, but I'll say in advance that I don't really like this (because it only works around the issue for this one particular case, rather than solving it more generally):

In `functional.h` add:
```C++
template <typename Arg> inline object functional_arg_cast(Arg &&arg) {
    constexpr return_value_policy rvp = is_copy_constructible<remove_reference_t<Arg>>::value
        ? return_value_policy::automatic_reference
        : return_value_policy::reference;
    return cast(std::forward<Arg>(arg), rvp);
}
```
then amend the Python function call in the `value = [func](...` lambda from:
```C++
            object retval(func(std::forward<Args>(args)...));
```
to:
```C++
            object retval(func(functional_arg_cast(std::forward<Args>(args))...));
```


But in terms of addressing the core issue, the main problems (mainly just dumping my thoughts on it here) look to me like this:

We need to be able to pass in an rvp to apply to each function argument in the lambda.  This is trickier than it seems, partly because passing just one value won't do: a binding might have multiple `std::function`s, and each argument of each `std::function` might be different.  So, for example, I need to be able to specify arbitrary set of policies for each of the A, B, C, and D arguments in:
```C++
m.def("foo", [](
    std::function<void(const A &, const B &)> f1,
    std::function<void(const C &, D *)> f2) { /* ... */ })
```
The nicest way I can think about to specify that is through the `py::arg` object:
```C++
m.def("foo", [](
    std::function<void(const A &, const B &)> f1,
    std::function<void(const C &, const D &)> f2) { /* ... */ },
    py::arg().arg_rvp([py::return_value_policy::reference, py::return_value_policy::copy]),
    py::arg().arg_rvp([py::return_value_policy::reference, py::return_value_policy::take_ownership])
);
```
(of course there's no reason you couldn't also name the argument, e.g. `py::arg("f1")` or `"f1"_a`, there, but that doesn't matter to this discussion).

The issue is then how to get that into the `std::function` type caster during argument loading.  One of the things I suggested as part of #864 was to change the type caster loading interface to be something like `load(handle h, load_options o)` (instead of the current `load(handle h, bool convert)`).  That would provide a nice way to get this setting through: it could just be something in the `load_options` struct (e.g. a `std::vector<py::return_value_policy> arg_rvps;` member).
(the reasoning being: `get_byte_offset()` was previously part of private API; now `byte_offset()` is public so we make it safe by default)

Hi @schoen!

Sorry, I worded myself badly here. This was meant as a bug report for the (python) acme client that certbot uses. It should, according to the acme draft, get a new nonce from the newNonce endpoint, and it does not. This can cause compatibility issues with some servers.

It's, off course, fine if you don't think this is an issue that needs fixing, but then it deserves a "wontfix" tag :-)

@hlieberman, it's not needed for the manpage or html docs.

It is needed for the PDF and might also be needed for LaTeX, but I can't remember without testing. If this matters for you, let me know and I'll look into it.
Another minor point for .deb and .rpm packages is it may be easier for people to keep Certbot up to date if we do what Google does with Chrome for example and add our repository to the repo list.

The `<VirtualHost *:80>` should not be inside a `<IfModule mod_ssl.c>`. Move the 2nd VirtualHost block outside of all `IfModule` blocks.
If it still does not work, there's another problem. The problem is difficult to deduce without looking at the full configuration but I can think of a few things.

- Use `apachectl configtest` to check the configuration
- If apache does not start (check with `ps` to be certain) and the ports are set to listening in netstat, another http server exists on your system.

Also, I think the there should be a `<Directory>` block somewhere for `/var/www/Portfolio-Webpage`; check if that exists. A missing `<Directory>` block sometimes causes 403 errors.
Is this still a needed thing? @bmw

> The tests currently don't work because the exception raised here https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/acme/acme/crypto_util.py#L134 and the code written here https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/acme/acme/standalone_test.py#L146 isn't executed.

That's because `test_it` is a pretty fragile test. Assuming I'm reading it correctly, we start a server in a thread in `setUp` and attempt to connect to it in `test_it`. The assumption in `test_it` is we fail to connect to it at least once.

To fix this problem, we could move `self.thread.start()` into the `except` block of `test_it`. We can create a bool named `started` before the `while` loop and set it to `False`. Then in the `except` block, if `started` is `False`, we only do `self.thread.start()` and set `started` to `True`, otherwise, we sleep.

Temporary solution that converts all setpoints to tuples:

```
def convert_setpoints(*args):
    """
    Convert setpoints to tuples, supporting multidimensional setpoints.
    Temporary solution to make setpoints work (see issue #627).
    Args:
        *args: 1D setpoint arrays. each successive setpoints array gains an 
            extra dimension

    Returns:

    """
    if not args:
        return tuple()
    else:
        first_arg = tuple(args[0])
        remaining_args = convert_setpoints(*args[1:])
        if remaining_args:
            remaining_args = tuple((arg,) * len(first_arg)
                                  for arg in remaining_args)
        return (first_arg, ) + remaining_args
```

It looks like we might want to also dive into the `_wrapped_cross_val_score` function and wrap around the `_fit_and_score` function

```python
def _wrapped_cross_val_score(sklearn_pipeline, features, target,
                             cv, scoring_function, sample_weight=None, groups=None):
    """Fit estimator and compute scores for a given dataset split.
    Parameters
    ----------
    sklearn_pipeline : pipeline object implementing 'fit'
        The object to use to fit the data.
    features : array-like of shape at least 2D
        The data to fit.
    target : array-like, optional, default: None
        The target variable to try to predict in the case of
        supervised learning.
    cv: int or cross-validation generator
        If CV is a number, then it is the number of folds to evaluate each
        pipeline over in k-fold cross-validation during the TPOT optimization
         process. If it is an object then it is an object to be used as a
         cross-validation generator.
    scoring_function : callable
        A scorer callable object / function with signature
        ``scorer(estimator, X, y)``.
    sample_weight : array-like, optional
        List of sample weights to balance (or un-balanace) the dataset target as needed
    groups: array-like {n_samples, }, optional
        Group labels for the samples used while splitting the dataset into train/test set
    """
    sample_weight_dict = set_sample_weight(sklearn_pipeline.steps, sample_weight)

    features, target, groups = indexable(features, target, groups)

    cv = check_cv(cv, target, classifier=is_classifier(sklearn_pipeline))
    cv_iter = list(cv.split(features, target, groups))
    scorer = check_scoring(sklearn_pipeline, scoring=scoring_function)

    try:
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter('ignore')
            scores = [_fit_and_score(estimator=clone(sklearn_pipeline),
                                    X=features,
                                    y=target,
                                    scorer=scorer,
                                    train=train,
                                    test=test,
                                    verbose=0,
                                    parameters=None,
                                    fit_params=sample_weight_dict)
                                for train, test in cv_iter]
            CV_score = np.array(scores)[:, 0]
            return np.nanmean(CV_score)
```

Ah ok upon looking deeper into this, it seems that PILs image `close` function states in the docstring:
```
    def close(self):
        """
        Closes the file pointer, if possible.
        This operation will destroy the image core and release its memory.
        The image data will be unusable afterward.
        This function is only required to close images that have not
        had their file read and closed by the
        :py:meth:`~PIL.Image.Image.load` method.
        """
```

I think having a text like this would be better:
```
    Player to feed stream data to. By default, VLC will be used
    if it can be found in its default location.
    This is a shell-like syntax to support using a specific player:
      streamlink --player=vlc <url> <quality>
    Absolute or relative paths can also be passed via this option
    in the event the player's executable can not be resolved:
      streamlink --player=/path/to/vlc <url> <quality>
      streamlink --player=./vlc-player/vlc <url> <quality>
    To use a player that is located in a path with spaces you must
    quote the parameter or its value:
      streamlink "--player=/path/with spaces/vlc" <url> <quality>
      streamlink --player "/path/with spaces/vlc" <url> <quality>
    Options may also be passed to the player:
      streamlink --player "vlc --file-caching=5000" <url> <quality>
    As an alternative to this, see the --player-args parameter,
    which doesn't log the custom player arguments.
```

This is more structured and has strict, unambiguous command lines. First the default value, then a simple player executable, then full paths and paths with spaces with different parameter formats, then player arguments and a reference to `--player-args`. I also removed the unnecessary and confusing wrapping quotation marks.

```

pid=0

function finish {
    [ ${pid} -gt 0 ] && kill ${pid} 2>/dev/null
}
trap finish EXIT

streamlink ${@:2} &
pid=$!

sleep "${1}"
finish
```

Note to whoever does this: one way to do it without having to sync the output messages back to the kernel is to make a custom private field, say _output_messages, which is either null or the list of outputs. Generally, it will be null, but it has a custom serializer that puts in the output messages. Since we'll never save changes manually on this field, the output messages are never sent to the kernel. But saving the output widget state to the notebook will substitute in the output messages so they are persisted. On instantiation, the output widget will look at this field for an initial set of output messages, and then clear the field.

> Is it possible to hint a short explanation, when/why a notebook extension is one time installed via pip ( or conda equivalent) and here via jupyter labextension install ?

The classic notebook and JupyterLab have entirely different extension mechanisms. In JupyterLab, an extension is a javascript package (npm package) that is built into a new build of the JupyterLab application. In the classic notebook, the extension system works with the python packaging system and that is the only way to distribute your javascript code.

@dhgary 
The sequence that worked for me (based on @ramkv post) was:
1. Uninstall JPype1 and remove all possible left over folders under lib/site-packages.
2. In a command window set JAVA_HOME as set JAVA_HOME=C:\Progra~2\Java\jdk1.8.0_181 (do not enclose 8.3 path into double quotes).
3. Install Jpype1 with --no-cache-dir option (pip --no-cache-dir install jpype1).

After that the test mini-program started working. Before that it was crashing python.exe with access violation at the same point as in your case.

I tend to believe that there is an issue with JPype1 setup/linking step when JAVA_HOME is not set or set to a path with spaces in it.

As soon as correctly installed/linked on my Win 7 JPype works with both JRE 7 and JRE 8 and I can use regular windows paths with spaces to jvm.dll
